Guide Destination Brief

Day 1 — Lyon

Thursday 4 June 2026 · Arrival & Embarkation · Ship departs 20:00
Day 1 · Lyon · Arrival
Embarkation · Ship departs 20:00
Welcome to Lyon ! There is nothing better than opening a brand new itinerary.

For the next eight days, we're charting a fresh course south along the Rhône — linking France's greatest food city to the landscapes of Arles on a route that's completely our own.

Tonight, we kick things off. We're on a spectacular ship, grounded in a city with over two thousand years of history, and backed by the best crew on the water, and la crème de la crème des Guides !!!
Pre-night: Boscolo Hôtel LyonEmbarkation: Quai Claude Bernard from 13:00Welcome cocktail: 17:30Safety drill before departureWelcome dinner: 19:00Ship departs Lyon: 20:00
Day 1
Arrival, Embarkation & First Hours Aboard
▾
The Arrival
Today the group assembles on the AmaKristina from three directions: guests arriving directly from the airport, those who spent a pre-night at the Boscolo Hôtel in the Presqu'île, and the Paris extension group joining with Michaela and Laura by TGV into Lyon Part-Dieu.
Logistics
Pre-night
Boscolo Hôtel · Lyon Presqu'île · airport transfers from CDG and LYS
Embarkation
13:00–15:00 · Quai Claude Bernard · Rhône left bank
Cabins ready
15:00
Safety drill
Required before ship departs · all guests · compulsory
Welcome cocktail
17:30 · Sun deck (weather permitting) or lounge
Welcome dinner
19:00 · Restaurant
Ship departs
20:00 · overnight sailing toward Tournon
Pre-night guests arriving June 3 independently — confirm airport transfer times with DMC. Luggage from hotel to ship is coordinated by . Guides confirm the handoff protocol with the Cruise Director on arrival.
The Week Ahead
A High-Level Overview — Weaving the Stories
▾
The Trip in One Breath
Grab a bike and take in the scale of Lyon at a fluid, relaxed pace. You'll pedal along the wide, engineered banks of both the Rhône and the Saône, cutting right through the heart of the Presqu'île peninsula. The ride takes you all the way to the Confluence, where the city's historic core sharply transitions into striking, cutting-edge modern architecture, giving you a complete look at how Lyon has reinvented its waterfront.
The Four Narrative Threads
  • Thread 1 — The River as a Roman Road. The Rhône was Rome's highway into Gaul. Lyon (43 BCE) was the Roman capital of three provinces. The Pont du Gard (50 CE) carried water to 50,000 people with engineering that astonishes modern hydraulic experts. Arles (46 BCE) was Julius Caesar's gift to his victorious legions, later a western imperial capital. We sail the same river, stop at the same cities, drink the wine they drank and eat the food they grew. The 2,000-year gap is smaller than it sounds.
  • Thread 2 — The Material World. The same iron mineral colours the Roussillon ochre cliffs, the Aven d'Orgnac Red Chamber, the cave painting pigment from Day 4, and the Baroque paintings of Rembrandt and Vermeer. The silk from Lyon's Jacquard looms gave the world the logic of the computer. The olive trees Van Gogh painted are the same species that the Greeks brought to Provence 2,600 years ago. Materials connect everything across time — the same stone, the same mineral, the same tree — and we touch all of them.
  • Thread 3 — Making Something. Every day of this trip involves making something with your hands: tasting wine like a professional, tempering chocolate, mixing natural pigments, paddling a kayak toward a Roman monument, painting a Provençal landscape en plein air. These are not demonstrations. They are participations. The difference between watching and doing is the difference between remembering and forgetting.
  • Thread 4 — What Each Generation Discovers. The 8-year-old and the 70-year-old will find completely different things on this trip — and be surprised by the same things at the same moments. The traboule passage that the Resistance used to evade the Gestapo is a secret corridor for a child and a history lesson for a grandparent and both reactions are completely true simultaneously.
The Day-by-Day Shape

Day 2 · Lyon — Roman foundations, hidden Resistance passageways, the silk loom that became the computer, the Mères Lyonnaises who built the world's food capital without credit. France as it actually is, not as postcards suggest.

Day 3 · Tournon — A private steam train through a volcanic gorge, a walk down one of France's most famous wine appellations, and the company that invented fine chocolate. Extraordinary things in an unassuming place.

Day 4 · Viviers — The smallest cathedral city in France, unchanged since the 16th century. A wine school inside a medieval castle. A prehistoric cave with a 1935 discovery story and a Palaeolithic painting workshop using the same pigments as 36,000 years ago.

Day 5 · Avignon Region — The richest single day geographically: ochre cliffs that coloured Renaissance painting, a kayak approach to a Roman aqueduct, the top of that aqueduct (access almost no one gets), and the world's most famous wine village, named for a pope.

Day 6 · Avignon → Arles — The largest Gothic building ever constructed, built to house seven French popes. A bridge that ends mid-river. Then the ship moves south and Arles appears: Van Gogh's city, Rome's arena, still in use.

Day 7 · Arles — Van Gogh's landscape, painted with his materials, in his specific light. A 12th-century limestone quarry turned immersive art installation. Olive oil from trees older than the Second World War, pressed 48 hours ago, tasted today.

Day 8 · Departure — Au revoir. The river stays. The looking comes with you.

Rabbit Holes 🐇 — For the Pre-Night
🌊 Wikipedia — Rhône ▾

The Rhône rises in the Rhône Glacier in the Swiss Alps at 1,753m altitude and travels 812km to its Mediterranean delta in the Camargue. It descends through the Swiss plateau, enters France at Geneva, turns south at Lyon where it is joined by the Saône, and runs through the geological corridor between the Massif Central (west) and the Alps (east) before spreading into its alluvial delta south of Arles.

The geological character of the river changes dramatically along the cruise route: north of Lyon the surrounding hills are granite (Beaujolais, Hermitage); south of Lyon through the Ardèche the banks expose volcanic basalt from the Massif Central; further south, limestone garrigue dominates. This transition from granite to basalt to limestone is visible from the ship's deck across the seven days of the itinerary and explains the radical differences in wine character between Beaujolais, Hermitage, and Châteauneuf-du-Pape.

🏛️ Wikipedia — Lugdunum (Lyon) ▾

Lyon was founded in 43 BCE as Lugdunum by the Roman general Lucius Munatius Plancus on orders of Julius Caesar's successor Lepidus. It became the capital of the three Gallic provinces — Lugdunensis, Belgica, and Aquitania — making it the administrative hub of Rome's entire northern European operation. Emperor Claudius was born here in 10 BCE. At its peak, Lugdunum had a population of 50,000–100,000 and housed the imperial mint that struck gold coins for the entire western empire.

The Roman theatre on the Fourvière hill (built 15 BCE, buried under a medieval neighbourhood for 1,900 years, rediscovered and excavated from 1933) and the adjacent Odeon are the most visible surviving infrastructure. The Musée Gallo-Romain de Fourvière, built into the hillside below the theatre, holds one of the finest collections of Roman provincial artefacts in France.

🎨 Wikipedia — Vincent van Gogh ▾

Van Gogh produced approximately 2,100 artworks in a career of roughly ten years (1880–1890) — 860 oil paintings, 1,300 drawings and sketches, and 150 watercolours. He sold one painting during his lifetime. He spent the defining years of his career in the south of France: 15 months in Arles (1888–89) and 12 months at Saint-Paul de Mausole in Saint-Rémy (1889–90), during which he produced over 300 works.

His letters to his brother Théo are among the most extensive primary sources on any artist's working process — 820 letters survive, covering colour theory, composition decisions, his reading, his health, and his relationship with specific landscapes. The complete correspondence is published in English translation by the Van Gogh Museum (Amsterdam, 2009) and is freely searchable at vangoghletters.org.

Guide Destination Brief

Day 2 — Lyon

Friday 5 June 2026 · Full day ashore · Ship departs 18:30
Lyon · 5 June 2026
6 tour options · ⚑ ship 18:30
Pack your sense of adventure, because today Lyon becomes our playground.

We are diving headfirst into a city built on secrets. Ever wanted to walk through a wall? This morning, we're slipping into the traboules — a hidden network of 500-year-old secret passageways built right through the city's buildings. We'll walk the exact hidden routes used by the French Resistance to vanish into thin air right under the enemy's noses.

Next, we're stepping into a workshop that feels like a time machine. You'll see the massive silk looms that didn't just weave beautiful fabric — they actually used punch-cards that accidentally invented the logic for the modern computer. Yes, really. The tech in your pocket started right here on a wooden loom.

By lunchtime, we follow our noses to the ultimate foodie wonderland: the legendary covered market that turned Lyon into the food capital of the world. To wrap up the perfect day? We leave the city behind and ride out into the rolling Beaujolais hills just as the afternoon light turns to pure gold.

If you thought you knew France, think again.
Old Lyon Walk Silk / Maison des Canuts Bike Tour Food & Wine Beaujolais Vineyard Bike Tour PM
AM · Tour 1
Old Lyon Walking Tour — Fourvière · Roman Theatre · Hidden Passageways · Pralines
▾
Why We Go Here
Step into the largest Renaissance district in France and watch the layers of Lyon's history pull back. On this walk, you'll slip into the traboules—the secret, covered passageways used by medieval silk merchants and French Resistance fighters alike to vanish into the shadows. From the Gothic details of the Saint-Jean cathedral to the historic paths winding beneath the Fourvière hillside, you are walking through the literal spine of early Lyon.
The Story
  • Lyon was founded 43 BCE as Lugdunum — Roman capital of all three Gallic provinces. The hill we climb (Fourvière) takes its name from Forum Vetus. Modern Lyon is built directly on Roman bones.
  • Emperor Claudius was born here in 10 BCE. The man who conquered Britain and gave London its Roman name grew up in Lyon. England's founding story begins in a city most English speakers have never heard of. ↔ all ages
  • The Basilica (1872–1896) was built as a literal vow — Lyon spared from Prussian invasion. Architect designed it for astonishment, not contemplation. The critic who called it "an elephant on its back" was not wrong. The architect loved the description.
  • The Roman Theatre (15 BCE) was buried under a medieval neighbourhood for 1,900 years — rediscovered in 1933 during construction. 47 houses demolished to uncover it. Still hosts summer concerts. Romans carved their names in these steps — some still readable. ↔ all ages
  • The hidden passageways — built by silk workers in the 16th century to carry fabric bales under cover. 300+ thread through private buildings. Some pass through five consecutive buildings before emerging on a completely different street.
  • During WWII, Jean Moulin — de Gaulle's personal representative — coordinated the entire southern Resistance from Lyon using these routes. Posed as an art dealer. Unified eight Resistance movements. Captured June 1943, tortured by Klaus Barbie for weeks, gave not a single name. Died on a train to Germany. The Gestapo never fully mapped this network. It defeated them for four years.
  • The praline rose — caramelised almond cooked in copper drums until the sugar blushes pink. Made in Lyon for 400 years. Does not exist like this anywhere else. The tarte aux pralines is Lyon's signature dessert. Every bakery's version differs. Every Lyonnais has a fierce opinion about which is best. ↔ all ages ✦ Magical Moment
Setting the Stage

The passageway entrance is intentionally unremarkable — an unmarked door in an ordinary wall. Load the Moulin and Resistance story before you open the door. Once guests understand that the Gestapo spent four years failing to map these routes — that these walls kept people alive — stepping through an unmarked door in a residential building becomes something else entirely.

The Praline Rose — why it matters

The praline rose is specifically Lyonnais in the way that the recipe, the colour, the copper-drum process are all local DNA. The American "praline" is a completely different confection. When you hand an guest a Lyon praline, you are giving them something that cannot be replicated at home and has been made in this neighbourhood for four centuries. Get the context in before they taste it. The history makes the flavour land differently.

JA Fun Bits
Activities for younger guests
  • Roman Theatre — the whisper test. One JA whispers from the stage. Others listen from the top row. 2,000-year-old acoustic engineering, still working.
  • Roman Theatre — graffiti hunt. Roman teenagers carved their names in these steps. Find one.
  • Passageway mission. Count every building you pass through.
Rabbit Holes 🐇
⚔️ Wikipedia — Jean Moulin ▾

Charles de Gaulle's personal representative in occupied France, Jean Moulin arrived in Lyon in 1942 posing as an art dealer under the alias "Joseph Mercier." Over the following year he unified eight separate Resistance movements — previously suspicious of each other and operating independently — into a single coordinated network, the Conseil National de la Résistance. He operated largely from Lyon, using its traboule passageways as escape routes and meeting corridors. The Gestapo, under Klaus Barbie, never successfully mapped the network.

Betrayed at a meeting in Caluire on June 21, 1943, he was arrested and tortured for several weeks. He gave not one name, rank, or address under interrogation. He died on July 8, 1943 on a train to Germany from his injuries. His ashes were transferred to the Panthéon in 1964 in a ceremony conducted by André Malraux — one of the most celebrated speeches in French history. Barbie was extradited from Bolivia in 1983 and tried in Lyon in 1987, convicted of crimes against humanity.

🎬 Wikipedia — Workers Leaving the Lumière Factory (1895) ▾

Shot on December 28, 1895, at the Lumière photographic plate factory on Rue Saint-Victor in Lyon's Montplaisir district, this 46-second film was among the ten shown at the world's first public cinema screening — held that same day at the Grand Café, 14 Boulevard des Capucines, Paris. Admission was one franc. Thirty-three people attended. Within weeks the Lumière Cinématographe was drawing crowds of 2,500 per day.

The Lumière brothers (Auguste and Louis) had developed the Cinématographe — simultaneously a camera, printer, and projector — in their Lyon laboratory. The film shows approximately 200 workers, mainly women, leaving through the factory gates at lunchtime. The camera operator was almost certainly Louis Lumière. The film is preserved at the Institut Lumière, still operating in Lyon on the site of the original factory.

🗺️ Atlas Obscura — Lyon's Hidden Passageways ▾

The traboule network consists of 315+ passages cutting through consecutive private buildings, allowing movement between streets without using the public road. The word derives from the Latin trans ambulare — to walk through. They were built primarily by the silk industry in the 16th and 17th centuries to transport fabric bales under cover, protecting silk from rain damage. The oldest known traboule dates from 1315. Some passages cross through four or five consecutive buildings before emerging on a different street entirely.

During the German occupation (1940–44), the Resistance used them as escape routes and meeting corridors — the Gestapo spent four years failing to map the complete network. Today most traboules in Vieux Lyon (5th arrondissement) are legally open to the public during daytime hours; residents are required by the city to maintain access. The doors are unmarked from the street — the only way to find them is to know where they are.

Logistics
Coach
08:15 at ship
Tour
08:45–12:15 · local + guide
Return
Coach 12:10–12:30
Gentle version
Starts lower city, skips Fourvière climb — split before departure
200+ steps on full route — pre-screen mobility before departure.
AM · Tour 2
Silk Experience — Maison des Canuts & Mur des Canuts ⚑ No Local Guide
▾
Why We Go Here
Cross the river and head up into the Croix-Rousse district to stand in the workshops that once drove the global textile trade. Here, you will see authentic Jacquard looms in full, rhythmic operation and discover a surprising connection to the modern world: how the complex punch-card system used by these 19th-century weavers laid the structural blueprint for early computer coding. It's a fascinating look at where old-world craftsmanship meets the dawn of technology.
The Story
  • The Canuts — 30,000+ silk weavers by 1800, largest craft district in Europe. The buildings here have 4.5–5m ceilings built for Jacquard loom height. Visible across the whole neighbourhood today — the architecture is an industrial memory.
  • The Jacquard loom (1804): punched cards control which threads rise. Charles Babbage saw a demonstration and modelled the first mechanical computer on this principle. Ada Lovelace — the world's first programmer — explicitly cited the Jacquard loom in her notes. IBM used punch-card systems derived from this method through the 1970s. Every smartphone on earth has a direct intellectual ancestor in this room. ↔ all ages
  • The Canut Revolt of 1831 — first armed industrial workers' uprising in history, 17 years before Marx's Communist Manifesto. Weavers held Lyon for 10 days. Their motto: "Vivre en travaillant ou mourir en combattant." Marx studied these revolts in detail. They appear in Capital.
  • The Mur des Canuts — 1,200m² trompe-l'oeil fresco. Every person in the scene is a real Lyon resident who posed. Jacquard looms visible in the background windows. Lyon has 150+ large-scale murals — more than any other French city. ↔ all ages
Setting the Stage

The loom-to-computer reveal is the unlock for this entire stop. The moment an guest connects the device in their pocket to a 19th-century silk workshop in this room — and watches that land — is the moment. A grandparent who used IBM systems and a 10-year-old who has never known a world without a smartphone arrive at the same astonishment from completely different directions. Sit in that for a beat.

Commentary Framework — guide leads, no local guide
Step-by-Step Guide · Maison des Canuts
1
On the coach (5 min): Point out the 5-metre windows. Those ceilings were built for machines that no longer exist. The neighbourhood is an architectural memory of an industry that changed everything.
2
Before entering: Set the stakes. 30,000 weavers. Largest craft district in Europe. Dressed Versailles, London, and Vienna — from this hill. And the loom they built accidentally changed the world more than their silk ever did.
3
At the loom (museum guide leads in French — adds in English): "The punched-card logic this loom uses is what Charles Babbage modelled the first computer on. Ada Lovelace said in her notes that it 'weaves algebraic patterns just as the Jacquard loom weaves flowers and leaves.' The device in your pocket goes back to this room."
4
In the museum (self-guided): Hit (a) why the ceilings are this height; (b) the Canut Revolt of 1831 — first workers' uprising in industrial history, 17 years before Marx; (c) the silk samples — what this hill produced for European royalty.
5
At the Mur des Canuts (group photo stop): Before anyone raises a phone — "Look at this as a painting first. Find one person who seems like a real person." Then reveal: every single person posed for the artists. The looms in the windows are a deliberate tribute.
6
On the coach back: The Canut Revolt thread — first time industrial workers anywhere picked up arms. Their demands: a minimum wage, the right to negotiate contracts. The rights we now take for granted. Started here.
JA Fun Bits
Activities for younger guests
  • Mur des Canuts detective: find looms in the background windows, find real residents in the painting.
  • At the loom: what language is the loom speaking? Hole = yes, no hole = no. That's binary. That's the same logic as every computer ever built.
Rabbit Holes 🐇
💻 Wikipedia — The Jacquard Machine ▾

Joseph Marie Jacquard demonstrated his programmable loom to Napoleon in Lyon in 1804. The mechanism uses a chain of punched cards — holes in specific positions raise specific threads, no holes leave them down. The sequence of holes is, in effect, a programme. Jacquard looms could reproduce any pattern of any complexity, replacing hand-controlled drawlooms that required a second operator to lift the correct threads by hand for each row.

Charles Babbage saw a demonstration of the Jacquard mechanism in the 1830s and used the same punch-card logic as the control mechanism for his Analytical Engine — the first design for a general-purpose mechanical computer. Ada Lovelace, writing notes on Babbage's Engine in 1843, explicitly cited the connection: "The Analytical Engine weaves algebraic patterns just as the Jacquard loom weaves flowers and leaves." IBM used punch-card systems derived directly from this technology through the 1970s. Every smartphone has a direct intellectual ancestor in this room.

👩‍💻 Wikipedia — Ada Lovelace ▾

Augusta Ada King, Countess of Lovelace (1815–1852), was the daughter of Lord Byron and the mathematician Anne Isabella Milbanke. In 1843 she translated an Italian article on Babbage's Analytical Engine from French and added her own annotations — three times the length of the original. These annotations contain what is now recognised as the first algorithm designed for execution by a machine, making her the world's first computer programmer by most definitions.

She explicitly described how the Engine could handle not just numbers but any system of symbols, and could be programmed to compose music — extending its scope far beyond pure calculation. Her notes were largely forgotten until 1953, when they were republished and recognised as foundational to computing theory. The Ada programming language (developed for the US Department of Defense in the 1980s) is named in her honour. Her explicit citation of the Jacquard loom as the conceptual precursor is the direct connection to Lyon.

✊ Wikipedia — The Canut Revolts ▾

The Canuts were Lyon's silk weavers, numbering approximately 30,000 in the early 19th century and concentrated in the Croix-Rousse district, whose 4.5–5 metre ceiling heights were built specifically for Jacquard loom dimensions. The Revolt of 1831 was triggered by merchants' refusal to honour a government-mediated minimum price agreement for woven cloth. Around 600 workers died in three days of street fighting before 20,000 army troops retook the city. Their motto — Vivre en travaillant ou mourir en combattant (Live working or die fighting) — became a founding slogan of the European labour movement.

The 1834 revolt followed the legal prohibition of workers' associations. It was more radical in character and suppressed more violently. Karl Marx studied both revolts in detail while writing Capital, describing them as the first class conflicts of the industrial era — 17 years before the Communist Manifesto. The Canut revolts are considered the origin point of modern labour politics.

Logistics
Coach
09:30 at ship
Guide
No local guide — leads
Audio guides
None in English
Rotation
25 weaving demo / 25 museum → swap after 30 min
Bus stop
Cannot stop in front — use Place Croix-Rousse or Rue Belfort
Return
Group photo at Mur des Canuts → ship
AM · Tour 3 & PM · Tour 3
Bike Tour — Lyon & Parc de la Tête d'Or
▾
Why We Go Here
For guests who want to feel a city rather than observe it. Cycling Lyon's riverfront to a 117-hectare park with a free zoo and 25,000 roses in full June bloom is a completely different register from the walking tours. The giraffes visible above the park walls before you even enter is one of those simple, unexpected images that no preparation quite prepares you for.
The Story
  • Parc de la Tête d'Or — 117 hectares (bigger than Hyde Park), designed 1856 in the English landscape style. Free zoo since 1857 (giraffes here since 1871, visible above the walls from outside). Rose garden: 25,000 plants, 350 varieties, International Competition annually since 1964 — peak June bloom. Botanical garden: 7,000 species.
  • The name — "Head of Gold": legend holds that a golden head of Christ was buried under the grounds after the Revolution to prevent it being melted down. 170 years of surveys. Nothing found. The park has been free and the search has continued in spirit ever since. ↔ all ages
  • Free admission since 1857 — a deliberate Lyonnais civic statement that public beauty is a right. Never changed in 170 years.
  • Model battleship competitions on the lake (1880s–1900s) — wealthy Lyonnais commissioned scaled naval replicas and staged mock battles on Sundays. Ended with WWI.
Setting the Stage

The giraffes above the wall before entering the park is the moment. Funny and beautiful simultaneously — one of Lyon's great urban non-sequiturs. Let people notice it on their own before saying anything. The pause before they work out what they're seeing is the beat.

JA Fun Bits
Activities for younger guests
  • Spot the giraffe above the wall — who sees it first before entering.
  • Rose estimation challenge: guess how many rose colours exist in the garden before counting.
Logistics
AM
Guides 09:40 · Departs 10:00 · Returns 11:30
PM
Guides 13:40 · Departs 14:00 · Returns 15:30
Age min
14 years
Setup
AMA Wellness Host fits bikes + safety brief
PM · Tour 1
Flavors of Lyon — Vieux Lyon & Les Halles Paul Bocuse
▾
Why We Go Here
Walk through the doors of Les Halles Paul Bocuse and find yourself in the culinary heart of France. This covered market is an institutions-heavy journey through French flavour, shaped by the legacy of the Mères Lyonnaises—the legendary women cooks who defined the city's robust style. You'll navigate past the counters of elite local producers to taste regional charcuterie, artisanal cheeses, classic pink pralines, and local wines.
The Story
  • The Mères Lyonnaises — female chefs who built Lyon's food culture before any famous male chef. Domestic servants in silk merchants' houses who opened their own restaurants when the industry collapsed. No formal training. Just ability.
  • Mère Brazier (1895–1977) — first person of any gender to hold 6 Michelin stars simultaneously (1933). Paul Bocuse apprenticed in her kitchen. When asked who taught him everything: "La Mère Brazier." The most celebrated chef of the 20th century was trained by a woman who grew up without electricity. ↔ all ages
  • The Ratatouille connection: "Anyone can cook" is the Mères Lyonnaises ethic, lived before it was a Pixar line. Thomas Keller (French Laundry), who consulted on the film, trained in kitchens shaped by the Lyon school.
  • Saint-Marcellin — runny cow's milk in a ceramic crock. Louis XI tried it in 1461 and ordered regular royal deliveries. Today: €2.50 at market. Same mountains, same recipe. ↔ all ages
  • Les Halles Paul Bocuse — 48 specialist stalls, peer-admitted. Where Lyon's finest chefs source daily. Quality enforced, not marketed.
Setting the Stage

The Mères story is the one to set up before the first tasting stop. The idea that the world's greatest food city was built by women with no credentials and no recognition during their lifetimes — and that the most famous male chef of the century openly credited one as his teacher — lands differently when the food is already in front of people. Tell the story before the bread and cheese arrive. Then let the Saint-Marcellin do the rest.

The Saint-Marcellin Moment
Lyon's Royal Cheese — 1461 to today, same recipe

If the vendor serves it warm — which a good one will — it runs at the edges when broken open. Warm, rich, slightly funky, made in mountains 80km from here, favoured by a French king in 1461 and unchanged since. The history and the flavour arrive simultaneously. No announcement — just the context before it lands on the bread.

JA Fun Bits
Activities for younger guests
  • The Remy Method: smell before tasting, describe before identifying. A real professional technique.
  • Junior Food Critics: after each tasting, one word for the experience — not the ingredient, the feeling.
  • JA version: water not wine, milder flavours — identical structure.
Rabbit Holes 🐇
👩‍🍳 Wikipedia — Eugénie Brazier (Mère Brazier) ▾

Born 1895 in a village near Bourg-en-Bresse, Eugénie Brazier left home at 10 after her mother died, worked as a domestic servant in Lyon from age 19, and opened her first restaurant on Rue Royale in 1921. In 1933 she simultaneously held three Michelin stars at her Lyon restaurant and three at her country property at the Col de la Luère — six stars total, the first person of any gender to achieve this. The record stood until Paul Bocuse achieved the same in 1965.

Bocuse apprenticed in her kitchen in the 1940s and cited her publicly throughout his career as his foundational influence. The Mères Lyonnaises were specifically female: women who had learned to cook as domestic servants in wealthy silk-merchant households and carried those quality standards into their own establishments when the silk industry collapsed. Brazier died in 1977. A museum in her name opened in the Col de la Luère in 2019.

🎬 Wikipedia — Ratatouille (Production) ▾

Released 2007, directed by Brad Bird. Thomas Keller of The French Laundry in Yountville, California served as the primary culinary consultant — Pixar's research team spent time in Lyonnaise kitchens and markets during production. The film's philosophy ("Anyone can cook") reflects the Lyon tradition of the Mères Lyonnaises and the broader French democratic approach to cooking as a craft available to anyone with genuine ability, regardless of social class.

The closing dish — a precisely composed ratatouille confit byaldi — was designed by Keller. The film is set in Paris but its cultural DNA is Lyonnaise: the reverence for humble ingredients elevated by technique, the primacy of seasonal local produce, and the idea that a kitchen's legitimacy derives from the quality of its cooking rather than the status of its chef.

Logistics
Coach 1
13:45 → Vieux Lyon → Halles 16:00 → ship 18:00
Coach 2
13:45 → Halles 14:15 → Vieux Lyon 16:15 → ship 18:00
Vieux Lyon
Brioche aux pralines + local chocolate
Halles
Charcuterie · cheese · macaron/dessert
JA
Water not wine · milder · same structure
Ship departs 18:30 — all coaches must leave Halles by 18:00. Friday PM traffic in Lyon runs heavy.
PM · Tour 2
Beaujolais Vineyard Tour & Tasting — Château des Ravatys
▾
Why We Go Here
Head north into the rolling, granite hillsides of the Beaujolais Crus to discover the serious side of this region's winemaking. Leaving the mass-market styles behind, we travel to the specific villages where the Gamay grape takes on deep complexity from the unique soil. You'll walk the vineyards, tour the cellars, and taste high-character, structured wines that showcase the authentic terroir of the region.
The Story
  • Beaujolais Nouveau was invented as a marketing concept in 1951 by Georges Duboeuf — the "race" from vineyard to Lyon café went global. The cheapest wine in the region became the world's entire idea of Beaujolais.
  • The 10 crus — Moulin-à-Vent, Fleurie, Morgon, and seven others — use the same Gamay grape on granite soils and develop genuine complexity over 15–20 years. A great Moulin-à-Vent at 15 years is indistinguishable from mature Burgundy Pinot Noir in blind tastings.
  • Gamay was banned in Burgundy in 1395 by Duke Philip the Bold — "a very bad and disloyal plant." Beaujolais ignored him. Some critics today consider that ban the making of Gamay's independent identity.
  • Gobelet vine training: each vine stands free, no wires, no trellis. A gobelet vine lives 50, 80, sometimes 100+ years. The oldest vines at Château des Ravatys were planted before WWII. Less fruit, far more concentrated. ↔ all ages
  • On the coach back — how a region's cheapest product became its global identity is a rich thread for the return journey.
Setting the Stage

The oldest vines on the estate are the physical anchor. Stop there before the tasting. Let everyone look at them — gnarled, low, seemingly modest. Then give them the age. Planted before WWII. Survived everything since. The wine in their glass tonight started growing in a vine that was already old when their grandparents were born. That scale lands physically, not just intellectually, when you're standing in front of it.

JA Fun Bits
Activities for younger guests
  • Junior Sommeliers — 4-juice tasting. Same glasses. Same vocabulary. Same sequence as the adult tasting next door.
  • Vocabulary challenge: describe each juice using only texture or feeling words — no fruit names. Silky? Sharp? Round? Heavy?
  • Molkky on the lawn during free time — Finnish skittles, fully adopted by France. They'll see it everywhere from here south.
Rabbit Holes 🐇 — good for the coach back
🍷 Wikipedia — Beaujolais Nouveau ▾

Created as a commercial category in 1951 by négociant Georges Duboeuf, based in Romanèche-Thorins. The "Beaujolais Nouveau race" — a competition to be the first to deliver the year's new wine to Paris — became a global media event by the 1980s. At commercial peak, Beaujolais Nouveau represented 50% of the entire appellation's annual production.

The commercial success simultaneously obscured the genuine quality of the 10 Beaujolais crus — Moulin-à-Vent, Fleurie, Morgon, Brouilly, Côte de Brouilly, Régnié, Chénas, Chiroubles, Juliénas, and Saint-Amour — which produce wines capable of ageing 15–20 years on granite soils. By the 2000s "Beaujolais" had become synonymous internationally with cheap early-bottled wine. The rehabilitation of the crus has been the major story in French regional wine since 2010.

🍇 Wikipedia — Gamay ▾

Gamay Noir à Jus Blanc is the sole red grape permitted in the Beaujolais appellation. It ripens early, produces relatively light-coloured wine with high acidity and strong red-fruit character, and thrives particularly on the granite and schist soils of the Beaujolais crus. Duke Philip the Bold of Burgundy banned Gamay from his domains in 1395, calling it "a very bad and disloyal plant" — the ban was a commercial measure to protect Pinot Noir's dominance. Beaujolais, outside Burgundian jurisdiction, ignored the decree entirely.

On granite soils, Gamay produces wines with a depth and structure completely absent from basic Beaujolais Nouveau. A mature Moulin-à-Vent at 12–15 years is regularly mistaken for aged Burgundy Pinot Noir in blind tastings. The 10 crus — Moulin-à-Vent, Fleurie, Morgon, Brouilly, Côte de Brouilly, Régnié, Chénas, Chiroubles, Juliénas, and Saint-Amour — each produce wines with distinct character from different underlying geology.

Logistics
Departs
13:45 · guide compulsory with JA group
Transfer
14:00–15:00 scenic · 259 Route des Ravatys
Adults
15:15–16:45 · winery + vineyards + tasting (4 wines) + snacks (bread, ham, sausage, cheese)
JA
15:15–16:45 · 4-juice tasting + Molkky · leads
Free time
16:45–17:15 · wine purchase
Return
17:15–18:15 · allow extra for traffic
Coach must leave at 17:15 firm. Ship departs 18:30.
Guide Destination Brief

Day 3 — Tournon-sur-Rhône

Saturday 6 June 2026 · Full day ashore · Ship departs 18:30
📷 #25

Guide Destination Brief

Day 3 — Tournon-sur-Rhône

Day 3 — Tournon-sur-Rhône

Guide Destination Brief

Day 3 — Tournon-sur-Rhône

Saturday 6 June 2026 · Full day ashore · Ship departs 18:30
Day 3 · Tournon-sur-Rhône
Steam Train · Valrhona · Hermitage Wine · ⚑ Ship 18:30
Get ready, because Day 3 takes us deep into wild landscapes and world-class craftsmanship.

This morning, the historic Chemin de Fer du Vivarais steam train runs for us alone. No crowds, no public schedules — just our group tracking through a spectacular volcanic gorge. There are no roads down here; the only way to see this wild canyon, carved out of ancient once-liquid basalt rock, is on these private tracks. It feels like stepping straight into an adventure movie.

This afternoon, we transition from rugged nature to the sun-drenched hillsides of the Rhône Valley — the birthplace of legendary Syrah. Our destination is a masterclass in flavour: the workshop that completely rewrote the rules of what chocolate could be. A deep-dive into the secrets behind the ultimate 70% Guanaja, from bean to bar.
AM: Private Steam Train — ExclusivePM: Chapel Hike + Cité du Chocolat ValrhonaPM: Château de Tournon + Cave de Tain Wine⚑ Ship departs 18:30
AM · Tour 1 · Exclusive
Steam Train — Vivarais Railway & Doux Gorge Expedition
▾
Why We Go Here
The Chemin de Fer du Vivarais is one of the last operational steam railways in France. This morning it runs for our group alone — no other passengers. The Doux gorge it passes through is one of the most dramatic volcanic landscapes in France: basalt walls rising 200 metres on both sides, carved by water over millions of years. This access does not exist any other way.
The Story
  • Built 1886–1891 to carry goods — silk, livestock, minerals — between the Ardèche plateau and the Rhône valley. For decades the railway was the only connection many Ardèche villages had with the outside world.
  • The Doux gorge was carved through volcanic basalt deposited by the Massif Central — one of France's most geologically complex regions, volcanically active within human memory. The black walls you pass through were once liquid rock. ↔ all ages
  • The locomotive burns wood, not coal — traditional for this region. The open-air vintage carriages are fully sensory: wind, noise, smoke, the smell of burning wood. You cannot get this in an air-conditioned coach.
  • Robert Louis Stevenson walked this exact region in 1878 with a donkey named Modestine, writing Travels with a Donkey in the Cévennes. He described the Vivarais as "a country of sudden contrasts" — gorges appearing without warning, landscape transformed within minutes.
  • Several tunnels en route — the transition from the dramatic open gorge to complete sudden darkness and back is one of the most reliably delightful moments of the trip. Every age group reacts the same way. ↔ all ages
The Rabbit Hole — Guanaja
  • Guanaja 70% is Valrhona's flagship dark chocolate, created in 1986 — the world's first 70% cocoa dark chocolate bar. Before Guanaja, the idea that a bar could be marketed at 70% cocoa and eaten for pleasure was considered uncommercial. It changed the category.
  • The name: Christopher Columbus encountered cacao on the island of Guanaja (off Honduras) in 1502 — his first contact with chocolate. Valrhona named the bar for that moment of first contact. Guests are holding the same starting point, 500 years later.
  • The bitterness is engineered: Guanaja's distinctive long, dry finish comes from a specific blend of Trinidad and Caribbean criollo beans, roasted to highlight tannins rather than suppress them. Most mass-market chocolate suppresses bitterness with excess sugar. Valrhona amplified it and called it complexity — and proved the market would follow.
  • Ask guests in the tasting: where does the bitterness arrive — front, middle, or finish? Most notice it late. That delay is the roast profile. It's the same question a sommelier asks about tannins in wine. ↔ all ages
div>
Setting the Stage

The exclusivity is the frame. Before boarding, one sentence: "This train runs for us alone this morning. The normal public service will run later. For now, this railway — built 135 years ago to carry silk from the Ardèche — belongs to us." A steam engine at full noise in a volcanic canyon requires no additional commentary.

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Tunnel count mission: how many tunnels total? Who estimates the longest one (in seconds) most accurately?
  • The Doux river hunt: visible far below in certain gorge sections — spot it before it disappears around a bend.
  • Smoke trail game: look back immediately after each tunnel. The steam trail hangs in the basalt gorge for a surprisingly long time.
Rabbit Holes 🐇
🚂 Wikipedia — Chemin de Fer du Vivarais ▾

The metre-gauge Vivarais railway was built 1886–1891 to connect the Rhône valley at Tournon to the Ardèche plateau at Lamastre, serving agricultural and mining communities along the Doux river gorge. At operational peak it carried 300,000 passengers and 100,000 tonnes of freight annually — a lifeline for isolated communities in terrain where road building was effectively impossible. The Doux gorge section runs through basalt deposited by ancient Massif Central volcanism, carved by the river into a canyon with walls rising 200m on both sides.

The line closed to regular service in 1968 but was saved by a volunteer association and reopened as a heritage railway in 1969 — one of the first such preservation efforts in France. The steam locomotives in operation are original to the line, maintained entirely by volunteers. Maximum speed on the gorge section: 25km/h. The departure is a private operation — no other passengers on the train.

📖 Wikipedia — Travels with a Donkey in the Cévennes ▾

Robert Louis Stevenson walked approximately 200km through the Cévennes and Vivarais region in September–October 1878, aged 28, with a donkey named Modestine purchased in Le Monastier-sur-Gazeille for 65 francs and a glass of brandy. The resulting book, published 1879, is widely considered one of the founding texts of travel writing as a literary form distinct from expedition memoir.

Stevenson was in poor health and processing a difficult love affair; the journey was partly physical, partly psychological. His descriptions of the Vivarais — the sudden geological transitions, the intensity of the volcanic landscape, the quality of silence in the gorges — remain the most precise English-language account of this region. The GR70 long-distance walking trail is now known as the Chemin de Stevenson and follows his route. Modestine was sold at the end of the journey for 35 francs; Stevenson wrote that parting from her was "like an affection."

Logistics
Coach
08:15 · departs ship · ~15-min drive to station
Station
Tournon Saint-Jean · 10-min transfer from ship · arrive 30 min before departure
Train departs
10:00 from Tournon Saint-Jean station
Train
Private departure — no other passengers
Route
Tournon Saint-Jean → Colombier-le-Vieux (~25 min up gorge) · train turns and returns · total ~1h55
Carriages
Open-air vintage · light layer recommended even in June
Return
12:30–13:00 · lunch aboard ship
No flip-flops on the moving train. Weather causing carriage closure is rare but possible — closed vintage carriages are available as fallback, still excellent.
PM · Tour 1
Chapel Hike & Cité du Chocolat Valrhona
▾
Why We Go Here
The hike down the Hermitage hill takes guests through one of France's most celebrated wine appellations — Syrah vines planted in pure granite producing wine at over €1,000 a bottle. Then Valrhona, founded in this town in 1922: the company that single-handedly invented fine chocolate as a category. The Bean to Bar workshop gives access to professional technique; the JA chocolate pop goes home as a physical memory of the afternoon. ✦ Magical Moment: the chocolate pop
The Story
  • The Hermitage hill: 137 hectares of south-facing granite, almost entirely Syrah. Romans planted vines here — Pliny the Elder listed Hermitage wine in the 1st century CE. The chapel at the top (1235) was built by Gaspard de Stérimberg, a Crusader knight who retired here to tend vines. The estate sold today by Paul Jaboulet Aîné as "La Chapelle" traces its name to that knight.
  • Valrhona (founded 1922): pastry chef Albéric Guironnet wanted France's finest chocolate, found industrial production too inconsistent, built his own facility here. The name combines Vallée du Rhône with Rhône — deliberately local.
  • Guanaja 70% (1986): the world's first commercially sold 70% dark chocolate. Before it, commercial dark was 50–55% and considered unpleasantly harsh. Valrhona proved that managed bitterness is complexity. The global fine chocolate industry dates its modern era from that single bar.
  • Grand Crus of cacao — terroir applied to chocolate: Manjari (Madagascar — bright red berry acidity, sharp); Jivara (Ecuador — milky, caramel, round); Guanaja (blend — intensely bitter, 60-second finish). Same principle as wine appellations, different continents. ↔ all ages
  • Tempering: liquid chocolate cooled precisely to 28–29°C then raised to 31–32°C working temperature. 1°C variance = dull surface, no snap, eventual bloom. Professional chocolatiers do this by touch and sight alone after years of practice.
  • Conching discovered accidentally (1879): Rudolph Lindt left his machine running all weekend. Returned to find the smoothest chocolate he'd ever made. Kept the accident secret for 18 years before selling the technique.
Setting the Stage

The Hermitage hill descent is the setup for the chocolate. The vines you walk through produce wine that sells for over €1,000 a bottle — grown in granite with almost no soil, no irrigation, maximum stress. Say this as you descend: stress and limitation produce genius. Then walk into Valrhona and show how the same principle applies to cacao. The afternoon tells one continuous story: what happens when extraordinary conditions are applied to something grown slowly.

The Chocolate Pop — Magical Moment
A Technique That Takes Years — Made Today

The chocolate pop made by each JA in the workshop is the tangible memory of the afternoon. Correctly tempered, it snaps cleanly and melts smoothly on the tongue. It is the same technique used by Valrhona's professional pastry clients. The JA made this with their hands, in the building where French pastry history was made. That context — offered before the pop is wrapped to go home — transforms it from a craft activity into something they will explain to every person who asks about the trip.

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Origin tasting challenge: before being told which cacao origin they're tasting, describe it in three words — texture, feeling, or experience only. No flavour names.
  • The snap test: correctly tempered chocolate makes a sharp, clean sound when broken. Softly crumbling = bad tempering. By the end of the workshop, every JA can hear the difference.
  • The 60-second test: after tasting Guanaja, time how long the flavour lasts. 60+ seconds = exceptional. Most commercial chocolate: under 10 seconds. The gap is usually audibly surprising.
Rabbit Holes 🐇
🍫 Wikipedia — Valrhona ▾

Founded in 1922 in Tain-l'Hermitage by pastry chef Albéric Guironnet, who wanted to create France's finest chocolate but found commercial production too inconsistent for serious pastry work. The name combines Vallée du Rhône and Rhône. Guironnet's founding principle — controlling cacao sourcing, roasting, and production in-house — established the template for the artisan chocolate industry. The Cité du Chocolat educational centre opened in Tain-l'Hermitage in 2013 and hosts working pastry professionals as well as visitors.

Valrhona's 1986 launch of the Guanaja 70% bar was the first commercially available dark chocolate above 65% cacao — before it, commercial dark was 50–55% and widely considered too harsh for retail. The company pioneered applying wine's Grand Cru terroir concept to cacao: Manjari (Madagascar), Jivara (Ecuador), and Guanaja (blend) each have distinct flavour profiles traceable to their geographic origin. Valrhona's Ensemble ethical sourcing programme — paying above-market prices for quality-certified cacao — predated most Fair Trade frameworks by a decade in several producing markets.

⛷️ Wikipedia — Hermitage AOC ▾

137 hectares on a single south-facing granite hill above the Rhône at Tain-l'Hermitage — one of the smallest prestigious appellations in France by area. The hill has been cultivated continuously since Roman times; Pliny the Elder listed Hermitage wine in his 1st-century CE catalogue of great Gallic wines. The dominant red variety is Syrah planted in pure granite without limestone, producing wines with characteristic black pepper, violet, olive, and cured-meat notes, with exceptional ageing potential (20–40 years for top vintages).

The chapel at the summit was built in 1235 by Gaspard de Stérimberg, a crusader knight who retired to the hill after the Albigensian Crusade to tend vines and pray. Paul Jaboulet Aîné's "La Chapelle" — named for this chapel — is the appellation's most celebrated single-vineyard wine. The hill is divisible into specific lieux-dits (Le Méal, Les Bessards, L'Hermite) with distinct geological and exposure characteristics, producing wines as differentiated as Burgundy's premier crus.

Logistics
Coach
13:45 · all guests
Hike
14:30–15:30 · summit to Tain-l'Hermitage · easy downhill, no technical sections
Cité du Chocolat
15:45 arrival
Adults Gr. 1
Bean to Bar 16:00–16:45 Salle 1 · Cité visit 16:45–17:45
Adults Gr. 2
Bean to Bar 16:00–16:45 Salle 2 · Cité visit 16:45–17:45
JA Gr. 1
Choc Pop/Tablette 16:00–16:30 · Cité 16:30–17:45
JA Gr. 2
Cité 16:00–16:45 · Choc Pop 16:45–17:15
Return
17:45–18:15 · Ship 18:30
Close-toe shoes required for the chocolate workshop — the supplier enforces this. Brief guests the evening before departure day.
PM · Tour 2
Château de Tournon & Cave de Tain — Hermitage Wine Tasting
▾
Why We Go Here
Travel to a 12th-century fortress built by the Lords of Baux, now home to a highly respected international wine university. Here, adults will learn professional sommelier tasting methodologies over a selection of six distinct regional wines. Meanwhile, the younger guests set out on their own parallel exploration, uncovering the legends of the castle and the surrounding paths of Garenne Park.
The Story
  • Hermitage: 137 hectares of south-facing granite, producing Syrah (red) and Marsanne/Roussanne (white). Continuously planted for 2,000 years. When Pliny the Elder catalogued great Gallic wines in the 1st century CE, Hermitage was on the list.
  • Syrah from granite is fundamentally different from southern Rhône Grenache-based wine. Granite — iron, magnesium, no limestone — produces Syrah with characteristic black pepper, violets, smoked meat, and remarkable longevity. Same river, different rock, different wine entirely.
  • Cave de Tain (founded 1933): 300 member growers across Hermitage, Crozes-Hermitage, Saint-Joseph, and Cornas. Founded during the Depression as a survival mechanism. Today consistently produces wine rated among France's finest at accessible price points.
  • The Château de Tournon was built by the counts of Tournon in the 16th century on the site of an older fortification. It guards the narrows where the Ardèche mountains meet the Rhône. The terrace view — Hermitage hill directly across the river, covered in the vines that produced what's in the glass — is one of the great wine-tasting sightlines in France. ↔ all ages
Setting the Stage

Set up the terrace sightline before the first pour. Give everyone a moment to look at the hill, look at their glass, look back at the hill. "The wine in your glass grew on that slope. That hill has been producing wine continuously for 2,000 years. The Romans who built the Pont du Gard — which we visit in a few days — drank wine from this same hillside." Then pour the first wine.

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Count the vine rows visible on the Hermitage hill from the château terrace — each row has a different age, different yield, slightly different wine.
  • Parallel juice tasting: same glasses, same vocabulary, same sequence as the adult wines next to them. One word per tasting stage: colour, nose, attack, finish.
  • Château steps challenge: find the oldest stone and the newest stone in the building. How can you tell them apart?
Rabbit Holes 🐇
🍷 Wikipedia — Hermitage AOC ▾

137 hectares on a single south-facing granite hill above the Rhône at Tain-l'Hermitage — one of the smallest prestigious appellations in France by area. The hill has been cultivated continuously since Roman times; Pliny the Elder listed Hermitage wine in his 1st-century CE catalogue of great Gallic wines. The dominant red variety is Syrah planted in pure granite without limestone, producing wines with characteristic black pepper, violet, olive, and cured-meat notes, with exceptional ageing potential (20–40 years for top vintages).

The chapel at the summit was built in 1235 by Gaspard de Stérimberg, a crusader knight who retired to the hill after the Albigensian Crusade to tend vines and pray. Paul Jaboulet Aîné's "La Chapelle" — named for this chapel — is the appellation's most celebrated single-vineyard wine. The hill is divisible into specific lieux-dits (Le Méal, Les Bessards, L'Hermite) with distinct geological and exposure characteristics, producing wines as differentiated as Burgundy's premier crus.

🏭 Wikipedia — Cave de Tain ▾

Founded in 1933 during the Depression by 24 grower families who could no longer individually afford to produce and market their wines. The cooperative model allowed small growers to pool fermentation, ageing, and distribution infrastructure while maintaining individual vineyard accountability. Today Cave de Tain comprises 300 member growers across Hermitage, Crozes-Hermitage, Saint-Joseph, Cornas, and Saint-Péray appellations, covering approximately 1,200 hectares.

It produces some of the finest Hermitage available — from accessible-priced Crozes-Hermitage to a top-tier Hermitage range that competes with the appellation's single-estate wines. The Cave de Tain model has been studied as a template for quality cooperative production in Burgundy, Alsace, and other French appellations where small grower fragmentation was limiting quality and commercial reach.

Logistics
Coach
14:00 · rotates with Valrhona PM group
Cave de Tain
Guided tasting: 3 wines, led by Cave de Tain representative · 45-min tasting · groups rotate with château visit
Château Tournon
Walk through interior + terrace sightline
Guide
Local guide + guide
Return
17:45–18:15 · Ship 18:30
Guide Destination Brief

Day 4 — Viviers

Sunday 7 June 2026 · Full day ashore · Ship departs 18:30
📷 #38
Pétanque cochonnet in gravel, two boules beside it, ground level
Guide Destination Brief

Day 4 — Viviers

📷 #39
Aven d'Orgnac De Joly Chamber stalactites, looking directly up
Guide Destination Brief

Day 4 — Viviers

Sunday 7 June 2026 · Full day ashore · Ship departs 18:30
📷 #40
