Guide Destination Brief

Day 4 — Viviers

Sunday 7 June 2026 · Full day ashore · Ship departs 18:30
Day 4 · Viviers
Medieval City · Université du Vin · Aven d'Orgnac · ⚑ Ship 18:30
The Ardèche is France's great open secret, and today the team gets to unlock it.

We start by walking a cathedral city so small that almost nobody has heard of it — yet continuously governed since 475 CE. A pocket-sized powerhouse of history that feels entirely frozen in time.

From there, a castle built by one of the most powerful medieval dynasties in Provence — an active estate where we taste through the wines of the region surrounded by centuries-old stone walls.

Then we go completely underground. Into a cave discovered in 1935 by a lone explorer armed with just a rope and a lamp. This is where the narrative shifts from history to prehistory — where the kids actually get to paint with the exact raw pigments used by Palaeolithic artists 36,000 years ago.
AM & PM: Viviers Walk + PétanqueAM & PM: Université du Vin + Suze la RoussePM: Aven d'Orgnac Cave + JA Painting⚑ Ship departs 18:30
AM & PM · Tour 1
Viviers Walking Tour — Medieval Cathedral City & Pétanque
▾
Why We Go Here
Step into a corner of France that feels entirely frozen in time. Viviers is the country's smallest cathedral city, continuously governed since the year 475 CE. As you walk its quiet, car-free upper town, you'll find yourself surrounded by medieval and 16th-century architecture that has remained unchanged for centuries. The afternoon finishes in a shaded local square for a relaxed, authentic game of pétanque.
The Story
  • Viviers as a bishop's seat since 475 CE: when the Roman city of Alba (5km away) was sacked by barbarians, the bishop moved to this defensible hill above the Rhône and never left. 1,550 years of uninterrupted Christian governance from a town most visitors to France have never heard of.
  • Cathedral Saint-Vincent: begun 11th-century Romanesque, modified through the Gothic era. The tower was the navigational landmark for Rhône boatmen carrying goods downstream for centuries. The facade is a legible record of 900 years of architectural decisions layered on the same structure.
  • The medieval upper town: entirely car-free, essentially unchanged since the 16th century. The Maison des Chevaliers on the main square has one of the finest Renaissance facades in the Ardèche. The town is a living document of how a French episcopal city actually functioned for a millennium.
  • Pétanque invented 1907 in La Ciotat (near Marseille) by Jules Lenoir, who was too ill to use the run-up required by the older game jeu provençal. He played from a stationary position — pieds tanqués, feet together. Within a decade it had replaced jeu provençal across the south of France and begun its spread to 50+ countries. ↔ all ages
  • The cochonnet (the small wooden target ball) means "little pig" in Provençal. Nobody is entirely certain why. The metal boules weigh 650–800g. Throwing one with precision at 8–10 metres is considerably harder than it looks.
  • Pétanque is genuinely egalitarian: age, physical condition, and strength are irrelevant — precision is everything. An 8-year-old and a 78-year-old play on exactly equal terms. When a local stops to watch and offer unsolicited advice (which will happen), they are additional cultural context.
Setting the Stage

The pétanque square must be a real square where locals actually play — not a designated tourist area. When the guides play alongside guests, the game becomes real. The competitive instinct is universal. Every family that plays together will reference it for the rest of the trip. The locals who stop to watch and offer coaching are not a distraction — they are the living proof that this game matters here.

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Cochonnet accuracy competition: who can throw it closest to the marked spot? Establish handicap rules so every age has a genuine chance. Kids vs adults is always a crowd-pleaser.
  • Architectural time-travel: walking the upper town, find where Romanesque ends and Gothic begins on the cathedral. Find the oldest carved date on any building.
  • The bishop's question: you've been the bishop of Viviers since 475 CE. It's the year 900. Describe your week.
Rabbit Holes 🐇
⛪ Wikipedia — Viviers, Ardèche ▾

Population approximately 3,500. The bishopric of Viviers has been continuously occupied since 475 CE, when the bishop of the Roman city of Alba (destroyed by Visigoth raids) relocated to this more defensible hill above the Rhône narrows. This makes Viviers one of the longest-serving episcopal seats in France by unbroken tenure — 1,550 years without interruption.

The medieval upper town — accessed through a fortified gateway and closed to vehicles — is essentially unchanged in fabric since the 16th century. The Maison des Chevaliers on the main square has one of the most elaborate carved Renaissance facades in the Ardèche. The cathedral was begun in the 11th century in Romanesque and progressively modified through the Gothic period; the tower served as the principal navigation landmark for Rhône boatmen for centuries. The lower town expanded commercially from the 17th century onward but the upper episcopal quarter remained sealed by its topography.

🎯 Wikipedia — Pétanque ▾

Invented in 1907 in La Ciotat, Bouches-du-Rhône, by Jules Lenoir, who suffered from rheumatism and could no longer execute the running start required by the older game jeu provençal. Playing from a stationary circle — pieds tanqués (feet together) in Provençal — eliminated the physical requirement. The name derives from lou joc de boules a pès tancats. Within a decade, pétanque had largely replaced jeu provençal across southern France.

The Fédération Française de Pétanque et Jeu Provençal was founded in 1945. World Championships have been held annually since 1959. The game is now played in 50+ countries with an estimated 10 million regular players in France alone. Boules must weigh between 650 and 800 grams and measure 70.5–80mm in diameter; the cochonnet (target) is typically boxwood, 30mm diameter. The game's democratic physics — precision over strength — mean age and physical condition are genuinely irrelevant to competitive performance.

Logistics
AM Coach
08:30 · local guide + guide
PM Coach
14:00 · same format
Upper town
Coach to base — streets too narrow for vehicles · walk up
Duration
~2.5 hours total · medieval walk + pétanque
Pétanque capacity
60 guests max per 60-min session · groups rotate through during the walking tour window · coordinate split with local guide on arrival
AM Return
~11:15
PM Return
~16:30
AM & PM · Tour 2
Université du Vin + Château de Suze la Rousse
▾
Why We Go Here
The Université du Vin is not a tourist attraction with a grand name — it is a functioning institution that has trained France's professional sommeliers and wine buyers since 1978. The 6-wine tasting uses the same methodology taught to working professionals. And the château housing it was built by the Lords of Baux — the same dynasty whose ruined fortress we visit on Day 7, connecting two moments of the trip.
The Story
  • Université du Vin (founded 1978): 300+ educational programmes per year, from 1-day initiations to 2-year diplomas. Over 100,000 professionals trained since founding. Being certified here carries genuine professional weight in the French wine industry. Today's guests do what the sommeliers do.
  • The 6-wine methodology: colour (tells age, grape variety, production method); nose (primary = grape, secondary = fermentation, tertiary = ageing); attack (first impression on palate); mid-palate; finish; length. A working taster covers these in 2–3 minutes per wine. The tasting is slower and more exploratory — but the framework is identical. ↔ all ages
  • The Drôme wine region: Grignan-les-Adhémar (red, white, rosé — Grenache, Syrah, Viognier); Clairette de Die (sparkling Muscat Blanc, naturally sweet, low alcohol, predating Champagne methods by centuries — one of France's most underknown wines); Côtes du Rhône Villages. The 6-wine flight typically draws across these appellations.
  • Château de Suze la Rousse was originally built in the 12th century by the Lords of Baux — the same medieval dynasty who owned Les Baux-de-Provence (Day 7). At their peak, the lords held 79 towns across Provence and the Drôme, and claimed descent from Balthazar, one of the Three Magi. Their symbol — a 16-pointed star representing the Star of Bethlehem — is carved into stonework across everything they built. Look for it here. Look for it again at Les Baux.
  • Louis XIII destroyed the main Lords of Baux castle in 1632 with cannon fire because they had become too powerful to leave standing. Their properties scattered. The Drôme département eventually established the Université du Vin here in 1978. The castle that survived is now a wine school. History has a sense of irony.
  • The Garenne Natural Park (JA territory): the former lords' private hunting warren — garenne means rabbit warren in Provençal. Medieval lords maintained warrens as controlled food sources, the way we keep kitchen gardens today.
Setting the Stage

The Lords of Baux thread is the narrative gift. This castle and the ruined fortress at Les Baux (Day 7) were built by the same family. "In a few days we will stand in the rubble of what Louis XIII destroyed with cannon fire because this dynasty had become too dangerous to leave intact. Today we stand in what survived — and it became a wine school." That arc — extraordinary ambition, one ruin, one institution — is the story of the Rhône valley.

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Find the 16-pointed star (Lords of Baux heraldic symbol) carved somewhere in the château stonework. You will see the same symbol again at Les Baux on Day 7.
  • Defensive architecture challenge: why are the windows small and high? Why does the main entrance funnel visitors into a narrow passage? Every design decision was made to kill attackers. Identify three defensive features.
  • Parallel juice tasting: same glasses, same vocabulary framework as the adults. One word per tasting stage. Describe like a professional, not like a child.
Rabbit Holes 🐇
🏰 Wikipedia — Château de Suze-la-Rousse ▾

Built in the 12th century by the Lords of Baux as their northern Drôme stronghold. The Lords — at their peak controlling 79 towns across Provence and the Drôme — claimed descent from Balthazar, one of the Three Magi, and incorporated a 16-pointed star (the Star of Bethlehem) into their coat of arms, carved into stonework across all their properties. The same star appears at Les Baux-de-Provence on Day 7. The château survived the destruction of the main Baux fortress at Les Baux in 1632, passed through various ownership, and was eventually designated a departmental cultural property.

The Université du Vin, established here in 1978, was the first institution in France dedicated exclusively to professional wine education. It now runs 300+ annual programmes ranging from 1-day initiations to 2-year diplomas and has trained over 100,000 wine professionals. Its establishment in a former feudal stronghold, in a region known for its Drôme wine appellations, gives the institution an unusual combination of historical authority and technical credibility.

⭐ Wikipedia — Baux-de-Provence (Lords of Baux) ▾

The Lords of Baux were one of the most powerful independent dynasties in medieval Provence, controlling an estimated 79 towns at their peak in the 12th century. Their claim to descent from Balthazar, one of the Three Magi, was incorporated in formal heraldry — the 16-pointed star (Star of Bethlehem) appears on carved stonework throughout their former domain, including at the Château de Suze-la-Rousse (Day 4).

The mineral bauxite was named after Les Baux when first identified there in 1821 by geologist Pierre Berthier. Louis XIII ordered the demolition of the main fortress by cannon in 1632 after the last of the line fought against the Crown. The ruins visible today are the result of that deliberate destruction. The dynasty's northern Drôme stronghold at Suze-la-Rousse survived and is now the Université du Vin.

Logistics
AM Coach
07:45 · 1-hour transfer
AM Tasting
09:05–10:05 · wine expert · 6 wines
Château Walk
10:10–10:45
AM Return
10:45–11:45
PM Coach
14:00 · same format · return 16:45–17:45
JA (parallel)
Garenne Park walk + castle tour · guide compulsory throughout
Steps between the tasting room and château courtyard — brief any mobility-limited guests before departure.
PM · Tour 2
Aven d'Orgnac Cave + Préhistoire Museum + JA Cave Painting
▾
Why We Go Here
Go beyond the typical postcards to discover the true botanical heritage of Provence. You'll learn the strict geographic and altitude differences that separate rare AOP True Lavender from standard lavandin, explore traditional copper stills, and participate in a hands-on workshop to harvest and create your own traditional lavender sachet to take home.
The Story
  • Discovery: 19 August 1935. Speleologist Robert de Joly descended 53 metres on ropes into complete darkness, alone, with a single hand lamp. He emerged and reportedly said: "I have just seen the most beautiful things in the world." The cave opened to the public 1939. UNESCO World Heritage 2014.
  • 13°C year-round inside the cave, regardless of outside temperature. In June, guests step from 25–30°C heat into what feels like a refrigerator. The cold is one of the most immediately surprising sensory experiences of the trip. Warn guests before descending — it arrives without warning. ↔ all ages
  • Three chambers: De Joly Chamber (named for the discoverer — extraordinary formation density); Chaos Chamber (enormous limestone blocks fallen from ancient seismic events, each the size of a car); Red Chamber (calcite infused with iron oxide — the same mineral as ochre — creating warm amber tones).
  • Stalactite growth: approximately 1mm per year in temperate limestone caves. A 3-metre stalactite started forming ~3,000 years ago — when the Trojan War had just ended, Homer hadn't been born, and Rome was a small village on the Tiber.
  • The Chauvet-Pont d'Arc connection: 60km from here, the Chauvet cave contains the world's oldest known cave paintings — 36,000 years old. Discovered 1994. The anatomical precision and compositional sophistication stunned the art world: skills not equalled again until the Renaissance, 35,000 years later. The Préhistoire museum at Aven d'Orgnac contextualises this directly.
  • JA cave painting materials: ochre powder (iron hydroxide — the same mineral in the Red Chamber walls and the Roussillon cliffs on Day 5), charcoal, manganese dioxide for black/purple tones, twig brushes or hollow-bone blowing technique. Authentically Palaeolithic. Same chemistry, same technique, 36,000 years later.
  • The elevator exit: visitors walk in via the entrance path, exit by elevator rising the same 53 metres de Joly descended on rope in 1935. Installed 1965 to protect the fragile entrance formations.
Setting the Stage

The temperature is the first physical cue — and it's useful. Before descending, a single sentence: "In a few seconds you will step from summer into a space that has been 13°C since before the Roman Empire existed. It doesn't change. Only we change." Then open the door. Let the cold arrive first. The formation reveal follows naturally — nobody needs to be told to look up.

JA Cave Painting — Magical Moment
The Same Tools, 36,000 Years Later

Before the workshop begins, show a photo of a Chauvet cave painting. "This was made 36,000 years ago. The person who made it was probably a teenager, sitting in a cave in the dark with exactly what is in front of you now." The finished painting goes home. A piece of Palaeolithic technique, made by this child, today, in the Ardèche where the tradition began. That context — offered before the first brushstroke — changes what the workshop is.

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Stalactite age maths: the guide points to a 2-metre stalactite. How old is it if it grows 1mm per year? (Answer: 2,000 years — same age as the Roman Empire.) JAs do the calculation. Then point to the tallest formation and ask again.
  • The Roussillon connection (Day 5): tell JAs before the workshop — "The ochre powder you're about to paint with is the same mineral as the orange cliffs we'll visit tomorrow at Roussillon. Same iron hydroxide. Same colour. Different hillside." Draw the thread between two days.
  • Cave painting mission: don't paint what you think it should look like — paint the most important animal in your life. Palaeolithic painters almost always painted the animals that fed or frightened them. What feeds or frightens you?
Rabbit Holes 🐇
🦁 Wikipedia — Chauvet Cave ▾

Discovered December 18, 1994 by spelaeologists Jean-Marie Chauvet, Éliette Brunel, and Christian Hillaire near Vallon-Pont-d'Arc, 60km from Aven d'Orgnac. Uranium-thorium dating places the oldest paintings at approximately 36,000 years old — making them the oldest known figurative cave art in the world, roughly twice the age of the better-known Lascaux paintings. The cave contains depictions of 13 large mammal species including cave lions, woolly rhinoceroses, mammoths, and cave bears, with anatomical accuracy and compositional sophistication that was not thought possible for this date.

The cave is permanently sealed to prevent deterioration (Lascaux was opened to visitors in 1948 and was visibly damaged within a decade). A full-scale replica, the Caverne du Pont d'Arc, opened 2km from the original site in 2015. Werner Herzog's documentary Cave of Forgotten Dreams (2010) was filmed inside using 3D cameras under special ministerial permission — the only film crew ever permitted inside. The film is available on streaming platforms and is the best available visual preparation for visiting the Préhistoire museum at Aven d'Orgnac.

🗿 Wikipedia — Aven d'Orgnac ▾

Discovered August 19, 1935 by speleologist Robert de Joly, who descended alone through a natural shaft 53 metres deep on ropes with a single hand lamp. He had been searching for the cave after local farmers reported cold air rising from a fissure — evidence of a large underground air mass. He explored for several hours and emerged to say: "I have just seen the most beautiful things in the world." The cave opened to the public in 1939; UNESCO World Heritage status was granted in 2014.

The cave's constant internal temperature is 13°C regardless of external conditions. The De Joly Chamber contains formation columns up to 10 metres tall. The Chaos Chamber contains limestone blocks of 1–20 tonnes, fallen from the ceiling during ancient seismic events. The Red Chamber's colour derives from calcite infused with iron oxide — the same compound as the ochre at Roussillon (Day 5). The elevator exit was installed in 1965 to protect the entrance-shaft formations; it rises exactly the 53 metres de Joly descended by rope.

Logistics
Coach
12:45 · 1-hour transfer
Cave Group 1
14:15–15:30 · Group 2: 15:45–16:45
Préhistoire Museum
English audioguides available · self-guided alongside or after cave
JA Cave Painting
15:30–16:45 · max 20 guests · guide compulsory
Cave temp
13°C year-round — brief guests to bring a light layer · even in June
Exit
Elevator only — do not attempt to re-climb the entry path
Return
16:45–17:45 · Ship 18:30
Tightest timing of the trip. Coach must leave the cave at 16:45 firm. Ship sails at 18:30. JA painting group capped at 20 — confirm size with DMC before departure day.
Guide Destination Brief

Day 5 — Avignon Region

Monday 8 June 2026 · Avignon mooring · Full day excursions
📷 #51
Full face of Roussillon ochre cliffs, all 17 shades, full sun, deep blue sky
Guide Destination Brief

Day 5 — Avignon Region

Monday 8 June 2026 · Avignon mooring · Full day excursions
📷 #52
Inside the top water channel, guests walking away, valley through arches below
Guide Destination Brief

Day 5 — Avignon Region

Monday 8 June 2026 · Avignon mooring · Full day excursions
📷 #53
Lavender field in full June bloom, low wide angle, bee sharp in foreground
Guide Destination Brief

Day 5 — Avignon Region

Monday 8 June 2026 · Avignon mooring · Full day excursions
Day 5 · Avignon Region
Roussillon · Kayaking · Pont du Gard · Lavender · Châteauneuf
Day 5 spreads across five completely different landscapes and two thousand years of history.

One group is kayaking down the river, watching the massive Pont du Gard appear over the treeline like a rumour until it fills the sky. At the same time, others are walking the vivid ochre cliffs that once provided the literal pigment for Rembrandt's canvases. And for the ultimate view, we access the very top of the Pont du Gard — 50 metres above the river, inside the 2,000-year-old Roman water channel that regular visitors never reach.

By afternoon, everyone reunites. We head into the legendary valley of Châteauneuf-du-Pape, where the popes made their wine, and wind down alongside fields of lavender so precisely protected they hold their own official appellation.

AM: Roussillon Ochre PathsAM: Kayaking Collias → Pont du GardAM: Pont du Gard VIP WalkPM: Lavender MuseumPM: Châteauneuf-du-Pape
AM · Tour 1
Artistic Luberon — Roussillon & Ochre Paths
▾
Why We Go Here
Roussillon sits above the world's largest known ochre deposit — 25km² of iron hydroxide-rich rock producing 17 distinct colour shades from pale yellow through orange to deep purple-red. The same mineral pigment used by Palaeolithic cave painters 36,000 years ago, by Rembrandt and Vermeer in 17th-century Amsterdam, is here, in these cliffs, in these paths, in the painting workshop today.
The Story
  • The geology: the ochre formed 65 million years ago as marine sediments accumulated when this plateau was submerged under a shallow sea. Iron hydroxide concentrated in different densities creating the 17 colour shades — pale straw-yellow to deep purple-red. The same iron that makes rust orange. Here, concentrated over geological time, it produces something of almost hallucinatory colour. ↔ all ages
  • The global art connection: ochre from deposits like this ended up on the canvases of Rembrandt, Vermeer, and Rubens. The warm golden skin tones of Baroque painting, the glowing backgrounds of Dutch Golden Age interiors — physically traceable to iron oxide pigment from Provençal deposits. The same mineral, the same colour, in the Rijksmuseum and on this cliff face.
  • Peak production (1900s): 1,200 mine workers, 40,000 tonnes per year, supplying France's entire paint and textile industry. Synthetic pigments collapsed the industry between 1929 and 1960. The cliffs remain. The colour is unchanged.
  • The Aven d'Orgnac thread (Day 4): the ochre powder in yesterday's JA cave painting workshop is chemically identical to this cliff face. Same iron hydroxide mineral, same colour, same Palaeolithic use. Two days, one continuous material.
  • Roussillon village: one of the Plus Beaux Villages de France. Built from local ochre stone — walls, streets, and architectural details are the same amber-gold as the cliffs. American sociologist Laurence Wylie lived here in 1950 and wrote Village in the Vaucluse — still the finest English-language account of Provençal village life.
  • The painting workshop: natural pigments on paper — ochre powder, charcoal, earth tones from this specific landscape. Cézanne painted these cliffs with these pigments. The line from Palaeolithic cave painter to Baroque master to guest is a single unbroken thread of iron and intention.
Setting the Stage

Before the first brushstroke, one thought to plant: "What you're holding has been used by artists for 36,000 years. Cézanne painted these exact cliffs with this exact pigment. You are about to join a very long list." Then let them paint. The context makes the activity something different from a craft class — it makes it participation in a tradition.

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Colour count on the path: the official number is 17 distinct ochre shades. Walk the path and count as many as possible. Most people get 6 or 7 without looking carefully — the difference between pale yellow and warm cream requires attention.
  • Ochre on skin: rub a handful of ochre powder between the fingers. The orange transfers and feels like silk. Palaeolithic painters felt this every day.
  • Village colour count: in one street of Roussillon, how many distinct shades of ochre are used in the buildings? The village is built from the cliff it sits above.
Rabbit Holes 🐇
🟠 Wikipedia — Roussillon, Vaucluse ▾

The village sits above the largest ochre deposit in the world — approximately 25km² of iron hydroxide-rich Cretaceous marine sediment formed 65 million years ago when this plateau was submerged. Differential iron concentrations produce 17 distinct colour shades from pale straw-yellow through orange to deep purple-red. Commercial mining began in the 18th century; at peak production in the early 20th century the mines employed 1,200 workers and produced 40,000 tonnes per year, supplying Europe's paint and textile industries. Synthetic pigments collapsed the industry between 1929 and 1960.

American sociologist Laurence Wylie lived in Roussillon in 1950 and wrote Village in the Vaucluse (1957), still considered the definitive English-language account of Provençal village life. The village is classified as a Plus Beau Village de France. The ochre path walk passes through the former mining terrain — the formations visible are both geological and industrial, the result of water washing through extracted rock over decades.

🎨 Wikipedia — Ochre ▾

Iron oxide (Fe₂O₃) mixed with clay and sand, found across most of the world's geological strata. The oldest known human use of ochre dates to approximately 300,000 years ago at Blombos Cave in South Africa — the same mineral, the same colour, used continuously by every subsequent human culture. Heating yellow ochre (goethite) at temperatures above 260°C converts it to red ochre (hematite) — a transformation Palaeolithic humans discovered in their fires and then deliberately replicated to extend their colour range.

The warm skin tones of Baroque painting — the umber, sienna, and ochre that characterise Rembrandt, Vermeer, and Rubens — derive specifically from iron oxide pigments sourced from deposits like Roussillon. Ochre remains in active commercial production as an artist's pigment and is classified as among the most lightfast (colour-stable) of all natural pigments — it does not fade with light exposure over centuries, which is why Palaeolithic cave paintings retain their colour today.

Logistics
Coach
08:15 · local guide + guide · 60-min transfer
Museum + Painting
09:45–11:00 · natural pigment workshop
Ochre Path Walk
11:00–12:00 · introductory route · relatively flat
Roussillon Village
12:15–12:45 · guide introduction + free exploration
Lunch OYO
12:45–15:00 · guests explore independently
Return
15:00–16:00
Ochre stains fabric permanently. Warn guests to wear clothes they don't mind marking. Aprons provided in workshop but exposure on the path is likely.
AM · Tour 2
Kayaking — Collias to Pont du Gard on the Gardon River
▾
Why We Go Here
Approaching the Pont du Gard by river — as the Romans who built it approached the work site — is the only way to experience the monument in its original landscape context. From a kayak on the Gardon the aqueduct appears above the treeline as a surprise, grows for 20 minutes of paddling, and finally fills the sky as you pass under the arches. This approach is available to nobody who does not get in a kayak.
The Story
  • The Gardon river: Grade 1 in June — suitable for all ability levels including children from age 6. Clear, shallow, warm in summer. The garrigue scrubland canyon on both banks contains 300+ wild herb species — thyme and rosemary released in the June heat makes the river corridor intensely aromatic.
  • The approach: the Pont du Gard appears above the treeline approximately 20 minutes before arrival — first the top arches, then the full three tiers as the river bends. Brief the group before launching: "Stop paddling when you first see it above the trees. Float. Let it grow."
  • Under the arches: the mortar-free joints between stones visible from water level. Roman builders' marks on the lower piers — payment verification marks carved by quarrymen, still readable after 2,000 years. The scale of the arches from directly below overwhelms in a way the roadside view does not.
  • Wildlife on the Gardon: great herons in the shallows, European pond turtles basking on warm rocks (slide into water as the kayak approaches), cormorants on exposed stones, possible Eurasian otters (reintroduced since 2000). Most reliable wildlife encounter of the whole trip. ↔ all ages
  • Fragonard and Hubert Robert painted the Pont du Gard from the river in the 18th century — it became one of the defining images of the French Grand Tour. Every guest who photographs the monument from a kayak continues a 250-year tradition of artists responding to this exact view.
Setting the Stage

Before launching, one framing sentence: "We are about to approach one of the greatest Roman monuments in Europe the way the Romans approached their own construction site — by river, slowly, from below. When the top arches appear above the trees: stop paddling. Let it grow. You will feel it before you see all of it." Guides launch after the group.

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Wildlife officer roles: each JA is assigned a species — heron, turtle, cormorant, kingfisher, otter. First confirmed sighting of their species wins. Guide keeps score.
  • Under the arches: look up and count the individual stone blocks in one arch. There is a correct answer. Closest estimate becomes Chief Roman Engineer for the rest of the trip.
  • Reflection challenge: at the Pont du Gard, look at the water before looking at the bridge. Which appears more real — the monument or the reflection?
Rabbit Holes 🐇
🏛️ Wikipedia — Pont du Gard ▾

Construction approximately 50 CE, under Claudius or Nero. Part of a 50km aqueduct system carrying water from springs at Uzès to the Roman city of Nîmes (Nemausus), population approximately 50,000. The system drops 17 metres in elevation across its full 50km length — a gradient of 0.034% — achieved using only Roman surveying instruments: rods, plumb lines, and water levels. The Pont du Gard section, bridging the Gardon river valley, reaches 48.8m at its highest point — the tallest surviving Roman aqueduct structure.

50,000 tonnes of stone assembled without mortar, held by geometry and gravity for 2,000 years. The upper channel is 275m long, 1.8m high, 1.2m wide; its interior is coated with Roman hydraulic cement (opus signinum) and overlaid with calcium sinter deposited during 400 years of active water flow. The aqueduct ceased operation in the 9th century. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1985. 500,000 annual visitors; almost none access the top tier.

🦦 Wikipedia — Eurasian Otter ▾

Lutra lutra. Extirpated from the Gardon river system by the 1980s due to water pollution, hunting, and habitat loss. A recovery programme coordinated by the French Ministry of Ecology and ONCFS began in the 1990s with reintroductions from Massif Central and Pyrénées populations. The otter is now breeding in the Gardon system and is occasionally sighted on the kayak route from Collias to the Pont du Gard, most reliably in early morning.

Protected species under French law (arrêté du 23 avril 2007) and EU Habitats Directive Annex II and IV. Maximum adult body length approximately 90cm plus tail; weight 7–12kg. Diet primarily fish, supplemented by crayfish, amphibians, and water birds. Any sightings should be logged with the guide team for the regional wildlife monitoring programme.

Logistics
Coach 1
07:15 · Coach 2: 07:30 · stagger 15 min
Transfer
45 min to Collias · restroom stop en route
Min age
6 years old
Restrictions
Cannot swim = cannot kayak · no flip-flops · no restrooms on route · pregnant women excluded
Duration
~2h30 on the water · Collias → Pont du Gard
Return
11:25–12:30
Supplier cancels same-day for heavy rain, flooding, or thunderstorm. Kayak guide fee not invoiced on cancellation. Fallback: Pont du Gard museum visit works well. Have it ready.
AM · Tour 3
Pont du Gard — VIP Top Tier Walk & Lunch
▾
Why We Go Here
Gain access to a restricted area of the ancient world that independent travellers cannot reach. You'll ascend 50 metres above the river to step directly inside the highest covered water conduit of the Pont du Gard, walking the exact path where Roman water flowed two millennia ago. The experience is paired with an in-depth visit to the site museum and a relaxed lunch overlooking the monument.
The Story
  • Built approximately 50 CE under the reign of Claudius or Nero. Carried water 50km from springs at Uzès to the city of Nîmes, dropping only 17 metres in elevation across the entire route. That gradient — 0.034% — astonishes modern hydraulic engineers. The Romans achieved it with rods, water levels, and mathematics alone.
  • Three tiers: 6 arches at road level, 11 in the middle, 35 smaller arches at the top (the water channel). 50,000 tonnes of stone. No mortar — the joints are cut so precisely that geometry and gravity have held the structure for 2,000 years. Not one stone has fallen.
  • The water channel: 1.8m high, 1.2m wide, 275m long. Interior coated in Roman hydraulic cement (opus signinum) and overlaid with calcium sinter — 400 years of mineral deposit from the water. The layering is visible: older deposits are darker, more recent are pale. This is Roman water infrastructure made physical.
  • Capacity: 40,000 cubic metres per day — enough for a city of 50,000 people. Operated approximately 400 years before silting and the decline of Roman maintenance ended its use.
  • Builders' marks on the lower piers: quarrymen carved numbers and letters into each stone for payment verification. Still readable after 2,000 years. The workers who placed the stones above us left their signatures in them.
  • Fragonard, Hubert Robert, and dozens of Grand Tour painters depicted the Pont du Gard in the 18th century. 500,000 visitors come every year. Almost none walk the top. ↔ all ages
Setting the Stage

One sentence before entering the top tier: "Half a million people visit this site every year. Almost none of them walk where we are about to walk." Then enter. The calcium-coated interior of a 2,000-year-old water channel at 50 metres above the river requires no embellishment. The space does not require narration on entry.

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Arch count verification: 6 on the bottom, 11 in the middle, 35 on top. Confirm the numbers from the top tier. Each tier was built with different stone from different quarries — can you see the difference in colour?
  • Builders' marks hunt: on the lower piers, find a Roman quarryman's payment mark carved into the stone. It will look like a letter or simple symbol. That mark meant someone got paid 2,000 years ago.
  • Calcium maths: the channel deposit grew ~1mm per year. It operated for 400 years. How thick should the deposit be? Compare with what you actually see.
Rabbit Holes 🐇
🏛️ Wikipedia — Pont du Gard ▾

Construction approximately 50 CE, under Claudius or Nero. Part of a 50km aqueduct system carrying water from springs at Uzès to the Roman city of Nîmes (Nemausus), population approximately 50,000. The system drops 17 metres in elevation across its full 50km length — a gradient of 0.034% — achieved using only Roman surveying instruments: rods, plumb lines, and water levels. The Pont du Gard section, bridging the Gardon river valley, reaches 48.8m at its highest point — the tallest surviving Roman aqueduct structure.

50,000 tonnes of stone assembled without mortar, held by geometry and gravity for 2,000 years. The upper channel is 275m long, 1.8m high, 1.2m wide; its interior is coated with Roman hydraulic cement (opus signinum) and overlaid with calcium sinter deposited during 400 years of active water flow. The aqueduct ceased operation in the 9th century. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1985. 500,000 annual visitors; almost none access the top tier.

⚗️ Wikipedia — Roman Aqueducts ▾

Rome constructed over 1,000 aqueducts across the empire at its height, collectively delivering an estimated 1 million cubic metres of water per day to urban populations. The engineering principle is consistent: gravity flow via a precisely calibrated downhill gradient, typically between 0.03% and 0.3%, maintained across distances of up to 90km. The Pont du Gard's gradient of 0.034% is among the most precise in the Roman system — achieved using only rods, plumb lines, and water-level instruments.

When terrain required crossing a valley the Romans built the minimum structure necessary to keep the water channel at the required height — hence the three tiers of the Pont du Gard. The technology was not significantly improved until pressurised iron pipe systems were introduced in the 19th century.

Logistics
Coach 1
08:15 · Coach 2: 09:30
VIP access
Top-tier water channel · Pont du Gard site guides lead · guide accompanies
Accessibility
80+ stairs required — not accessible for guests with mobility limitations. Guests who cannot complete the walk should be directed to museum only. Brief guests the evening before.
Museum
English audioguides available · self-guided
Lunch
Les Terrasses du Pont du Gard · monument visible from terrace
Return
15:00–16:00
PM · Tour 1
Lavender Museum — Workshop & Tasting
▾
Why We Go Here
Almost everything sold as "lavender" outside specialised suppliers is lavandin — a hybrid that grows at lower altitude and smells noticeably different from the real thing. The AOP Lavande de Haute-Provence is one of France's most protected agricultural designations, requiring cultivation above 800m altitude, and the oil from these fields ends up in the finest perfumes in the world. The sachet workshop uses the same plant as Chanel No. 5. ✦ Magical Moment: the sachet to take home
The Story
  • True lavender (Lavandula angustifolia): grows above 800m altitude, flowers once per year, produces extraordinary fragrance complexity. Lavandin (Lavandula × intermedia): a hybrid growing at lower altitude, more abundant, stronger but less complex. If it's in a floor cleaner or air freshener — lavandin. If it's in a luxury perfume — true lavender. Most people have never smelled the real thing. ↔ all ages
  • AOP Lavande de Haute-Provence: one of France's most restrictive agricultural appellations. Only ~200 producers qualify. Yield: ~120–150kg of flowers per litre of essential oil. At wholesale, genuine AOP lavender oil costs €200–400 per kg.
  • The lavender landscape is more modern than it looks: the purple fields that define every Provence postcard were planted primarily in the 1920s–50s to supply the Grasse perfume industry. Van Gogh didn't paint lavender fields. Cézanne didn't paint lavender fields. The "timeless Provençal lavender" is often younger than the people photographing it.
  • Grasse (70km east): holds 70% of global natural fragrance production capacity — world perfume capital since the 17th century. Chanel, Dior, and Guerlain source specific flower essences from Provence. The sachet made today uses the same plant that ends up in some of the most expensive perfumes in the world.
  • Historical use: Romans cleaned wounds with lavender, medieval monasteries grew it as medicine, Renaissance courts used it to scent bed linens. The technique — dry the flowers, contain them, crush slightly to release oil — is unchanged in 2,000 years.
Setting the Stage

The true lavender vs lavandin reveal is the day's key sensory moment. Before the tasting: hand each guest one stem of each without saying which is which. Ask them to smell both and say which is more complex, which is sharper, which they prefer. Then reveal. Watch what happens when the gap between expectation and reality arrives in one breath.

The Sachet — Magical Moment
A Scent Memory to Open in January

The sachet made today will smell like this afternoon for 2–3 years. When a drawer is opened at home in January and this scent arrives — the June lavender fields, the Provençal afternoon, this specific moment — that is a scent memory, the most durable form of recall the human brain has. Tell them this before they fill the sachet. Then the sachet is not a craft activity. It is a vessel.

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Blind smell test: eyes closed, two stems in each hand. Which is true lavender, which is lavandin? Describe the difference before being told the answer.
  • Garden scavenger hunt: find lavender, rosemary, and thyme. Pick one leaf of each and scratch the surface. These three plants are why the region from here south smells the way it does.
  • Sachet purpose: the sachet goes home. Before making it — "When you open a drawer in winter and smell this, where will you be in your memory?" Make it for that moment.
Rabbit Holes 🐇
💜 Wikipedia — Lavandula ▾

The genus Lavandula contains approximately 47 species in the mint family (Lamiaceae), native to the Mediterranean basin, Macaronesia, and parts of southern Asia. The two species relevant to Provençal production are Lavandula angustifolia (true lavender — grows above 800m altitude, single unbranched flower spike, highest essential oil complexity) and Lavandula × intermedia (lavandin — a hybrid growing at lower altitude, branched spike, higher yield but lower fragrance complexity).

The essential oil of true lavender contains linalool and linalyl acetate as primary compounds; the ratio between these determines the fragrance character from sweet-floral to medicinal. The AOP Lavande de Haute-Provence — the protected designation — applies exclusively to L. angustifolia oil produced within a specific geographic zone in the Alpes-de-Haute-Provence, Drôme, Hautes-Alpes, and Vaucluse departments, above 800m altitude. Most commercial "lavender" products use lavandin — it is cheaper, higher-yield, and more robust, but chemically distinct.

🌸 Wikipedia — Grasse ▾

Located in the Alpes-Maritimes at approximately 330m altitude, 17km from Cannes. The perfume industry established itself in Grasse in the 17th century, initially producing perfumed leather gloves for the fashion trade — the existing tanning infrastructure provided the technical base for working with odoriferous compounds. As the glove trade declined, the industry pivoted entirely to fragrance production.

Today Grasse accounts for approximately 70% of global natural fragrance production capacity. The city's flower-growing cooperatives supply jasmine, rose, mimosa, violet, orange blossom, and lavender to the major Paris perfume houses. Several houses — including Chanel (for jasmine and rose used in No. 5) — maintain exclusive sourcing agreements with specific Grasse farms regardless of market price fluctuations. The Musée International de la Parfumerie in Grasse holds the most comprehensive collection of perfume history artefacts in the world.

Logistics
Coach
14:30 · 45-min transfer · guides needed · no local guide
Groups
Max 40 per group · 2 groups rotating through 3 activities
Museum
40 min · guided history of lavender cultivation
Sachet Workshop
20 min · each guest makes one sachet to keep
Tasting
20 min · min 4 items: honey, tea, biscuits, infused water
Free time
Garden walk + gift shop
Return
17:30–18:30
Confirm dietary allergies before the tasting — lavender products can trigger reactions in some guests. Alternatives available on request.
PM · Tour 2
Châteauneuf-du-Pape — Domaine Mousset Private Visit
▾
Why We Go Here
Step off the ship into the warm evening light of Arles, a city where historical eras sit directly on top of one another. Your walk takes you past the towering arches of the Roman Amphitheatre—still vibrant and in use today—along the ancient stone city walls, and into the courtyard of the Espace Van Gogh, meticulously restored to match the exact geometry and colours Vincent painted from his window in 1889.
The Story
  • Pope Clément V moved the papacy to Avignon in 1309. The succeeding popes built a summer residence here — the château whose ruins are visible from the vineyard. The pope's wine became the most famous in the world by proximity to power. Six more French popes followed over 68 years. The village's identity was sealed for centuries.
  • The galets roulés: large rounded stones deposited by the Rhône during a more energetic prehistoric era when the river ran much higher. They absorb heat during the day and release it at night, moderating temperature around the vines. The vines root deep to find nutrients below the stones — stress produces concentration. Pick one up in the vineyard: it is warm from the sun. At night, this stone is still giving warmth to the air around the vines. ↔ all ages
  • 13 permitted grape varieties — more than any other AOC in France. Grenache dominates (up to 80%), blended with Syrah, Mourvèdre, Cinsault, and up to 9 others. This complexity of permitted varieties reflects the breadth of the appellation's ambition.
  • The Château des Papes: papal summer residence, destroyed in the Wars of Religion (1562), only the keep remaining. The silhouette against the sky at sunset is one of the great vineyard views of Provence.
  • Domaine Mousset: one of the historic Châteauneuf estates, offering private access. Tasting covers 3 wines, led by a Domaine Mousset wine expert.
Setting the Stage

Stop in the vineyard before the tasting. Pick up a galet roulé. Hold it. Feel the warmth from the sun stored in the stone. "This stone was deposited by the Rhône 10,000 years ago when the river ran much higher than today. At night, when the temperature drops, this stone is still warm. This is the terroir. This stone in your hand is why the wine tastes the way it does." Then walk to the tasting room.

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Stone colour count: the galets roulés contain stones from different geological origins. Count the distinct colours. Each colour represents material from a different Rhône tributary source — the river assembled this floor from across the Alps.
  • Parallel juice tasting: same structure as the adult wines. Same vocabulary challenge. Same seriousness.
  • Château ruins sketch: standing in the vineyard looking at the ruined keep — draw or describe what you think the full château looked like when the popes used it.
Rabbit Holes 🐇
⛪ Wikipedia — Châteauneuf-du-Pape AOC ▾

The appellation covers approximately 3,200 hectares across five communes in the southern Rhône. It was the first French appellation to receive formal AOC status, in 1936 — the system was partly created in response to widespread fraudulent labelling in the Châteauneuf region. The appellation permits 13 grape varieties for red and white production. Grenache Noir typically comprises 60–80% of red blends, with Syrah, Mourvèdre, Cinsault, and others making up the balance.

The galets roulés — large rounded quartzite stones covering much of the appellation's vineyard floors — are glacially and fluvially deposited Rhône alluvium from a prehistoric period when the river ran significantly higher. They function as thermal regulators: absorbing solar heat during the day, releasing it at night, moderating temperature around the vines. The papal connection (the summer residence of seven Avignon popes was built here in the 14th century) gave the appellation its name and its early prestige; the château ruins are visible from the vineyard.

🏛️ Wikipedia — Avignon Papacy ▾

Clement V was elected pope in 1305 but never travelled to Rome — the city was politically unstable, factionally dangerous, and controlled by competing Italian noble families. He settled in Avignon, which was technically a papal fief (part of the County of Venaissin, owned by the Holy See rather than France). Six subsequent popes followed, all French, all remaining in Avignon for a combined 68 years (1309–1377). The period is sometimes called the "Babylonian Captivity" — a polemical term coined by critics including Petrarch, who lived in Avignon and wrote extensively about the corruption he witnessed.

At its height, the papal court in Avignon employed 500+ officials and drew diplomats, artists, merchants, and bankers from across Europe. The city's population grew from approximately 6,000 to 30,000 during the papacy. The Great Schism (1378–1417) followed the return to Rome, during which rival popes in Rome and Avignon simultaneously claimed legitimacy — at one point there were three simultaneous claimants to the papacy. The Avignon papacy fundamentally shifted the political geography of medieval Christianity and left the city with the largest Gothic palace complex in the world.

Logistics
Coach
14:30 · local guide + guide
Domaine Mousset
Private access · vineyard walk + château ruins + tasting room
Tasting
3 wines · estate expert from Domaine Mousset leads · light snacks included (salted crackers + olives) · JA: non-alcoholic sweet beverages
Return
18:30
Guide Destination Brief

Day 6 — Avignon → Arles

Tuesday 9 June 2026 · Ship departs Avignon 12:00 · Arrives Arles 16:30
📷 #70
Four remaining arches of Pont Saint-Bénézet from the Rhône, ending mid-river
Guide Destination Brief

Day 6 — Avignon → Arles

Tuesday 9 June 2026 · Ship departs Avignon 12:00 · Arrives Arles 16:30
📷 #71
Sacristan pastry in an guest's hand, golden morning light, green wall behind
Guide Destination Brief

Day 6 — Avignon → Arles

Tuesday 9 June 2026 · Ship departs Avignon 12:00 · Arrives Arles 16:30
📷 #72
Ship approaching Arles from the north, amphitheatre visible above rooftops
Guide Destination Brief

Day 6 — Avignon → Arles

Tuesday 9 June 2026 · Ship departs Avignon 12:00 · Arrives Arles 16:30
Day 6 · Avignon → Arles
⚑ Ship departs Avignon 12:00 FIRM
Day 6 marks a major transition on the river — shifting from the towering strongholds of the church to the open light and ancient arenas of the south.

The morning belongs entirely to Avignon: the largest Gothic building ever constructed, built by seven French popes who simply decided to skip Rome entirely. Then the ship moves south, the landscape opens flat and wide, and Arles arrives — Julius Caesar's city, Constantine's capital, Van Gogh's obsession.

The day culminates in walking into the restored courtyard garden of the hospital where Van Gogh recovered — standing in the exact space he painted from his window.

We cross two millennia in a single afternoon.
AM: Avignon Walking TourAM: Late Riser + BakeryPM: Arles Introduction Tour⚑ Ship departs Avignon 12:00 FIRM
AM · Tour 1
Avignon — Privately Guided Walk & Palais des Papes
▾
Why We Go Here
Stand before the sheer scale of the Palais des Papes—the largest Gothic palace ever constructed, built in the 14th century when seven successive popes decided to break away from Rome. You'll explore its grand, cavernous interior and stone courtyards before heading to the banks of the river to walk the remaining arches of the historic Pont Saint-Bénézet, the famous bridge that stops mid-stream.
The Story
  • Pope Clément V was French. Elected in 1305, he simply never went to Rome — politically too dangerous, geographically inconvenient, too many feuding Italian families. He settled in Avignon, which was technically a papal fief and legally independent of France. Six more popes followed over 68 years. Cardinals, ambassadors, artists, and bankers from across Europe all came here. Petrarch, living in Avignon, called it "the shame of Rome" — and kept returning.
  • Two architectural personalities in one building: Benoît XII built the north wing — austere, fortress-like, the statement of a pope who distrusted ornament. Clément VI built the south wing — elaborate, celebratory, the statement of a pope who loved art. The contrast between the two wings is a visible argument about what power should look like.
  • Scale: the Palais covers 15,000m² — larger than any cathedral in France. The Grand Tinel (banqueting hall) is 52m long. Walking through it, the ambition of the project becomes physically, bodily comprehensible. ↔ all ages
  • The Pont Saint-Bénézet: built 1177–1185, originally 22 arches crossing the full Rhône. Four arches remain — the rest destroyed by flooding over centuries. The song "Sur le pont d'Avignon, on y danse..." is known by every French person from childhood. The dancing actually happened on the island beneath the bridge — not on the bridge itself. A small historical irony that delights people.
  • The Avignon city walls: 4.3km, 39 towers, 7 gates — completely intact, built in the 14th century to protect the extraordinary wealth the papacy had accumulated here. One of the best-preserved medieval fortifications in Europe. The rampart walk gives a view that hasn't changed in 600 years.
  • Notre-Dame des Doms cathedral (adjacent to the Palais): seat of the Avignon bishops since the 12th century — predating the Palais by 200 years. The gilded Virgin Mary statue on the roof was added in 1859 and is visible from the Rhône bridge and the surrounding hills.
Setting the Stage

Before entering the Palais, stop in the Place du Palais and give people sixty seconds of silence looking at the full facade. Then: "Seven popes governed the Christian world from inside those walls. For 68 years, this building in the south of France was the political and spiritual centre of a faith tradition followed by hundreds of millions of people. Then the popes left. And Avignon became what you see now — a small French city that is very proud of having once been the most important place on earth."

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Two wings, two personalities: walk through both sides of the Palais and find three architectural differences. Which pope wanted to look more powerful from the outside? Which wanted to live more beautifully on the inside?
  • The Pont Saint-Bénézet question: it's 1177 and you're an engineer with no machinery, no steel, no concrete. How do you build a bridge 900m across a fast-moving river? What's the single biggest problem you'll need to solve?
  • Rampart tower count: 39 towers in the original walls. Count as many as possible from the rampart walk. Each tower covers the dead angle of the two adjacent sections.
Rabbit Holes 🐇
⛪ Wikipedia — Avignon Papacy ▾

Clement V was elected pope in 1305 but never travelled to Rome — the city was politically unstable, factionally dangerous, and controlled by competing Italian noble families. He settled in Avignon, which was technically a papal fief (part of the County of Venaissin, owned by the Holy See rather than France). Six subsequent popes followed, all French, all remaining in Avignon for a combined 68 years (1309–1377). The period is sometimes called the "Babylonian Captivity" — a polemical term coined by critics including Petrarch, who lived in Avignon and wrote extensively about the corruption he witnessed.

At its height, the papal court in Avignon employed 500+ officials and drew diplomats, artists, merchants, and bankers from across Europe. The city's population grew from approximately 6,000 to 30,000 during the papacy. The Great Schism (1378–1417) followed the return to Rome, during which rival popes in Rome and Avignon simultaneously claimed legitimacy — at one point there were three simultaneous claimants to the papacy. The Avignon papacy fundamentally shifted the political geography of medieval Christianity and left the city with the largest Gothic palace complex in the world.

🌉 Wikipedia — Pont Saint-Bénézet ▾

Construction 1177–1185, attributed by legend to a shepherd named Bénézet who claimed divine instruction to build a bridge across the Rhône at Avignon. The completed bridge had 22 arches spanning approximately 900m across the full river width. At the time of its construction it was one of the only permanent bridges across the Rhône between Lyon and the sea — strategically and commercially critical.

Repeated flooding destroyed arches progressively from the 14th century onward; the four surviving arches represent the Avignon-bank section. The chapel of Saint-Nicolas on the second pier dates from the 12th century. The song Sur le pont d'Avignon (documented from the 15th century) refers to dancing on the Île de la Barthelasse — the island beneath and beside the bridge — not on the bridge itself, which was never wide enough for dancing. The song is known by every French person from early childhood.

🏛️ Wikipedia — Palais des Papes ▾

Construction began in 1335 under Benedict XII on the site of the previous bishops' palace. Benedict XII (austere, reforming, anti-nepotist) built the northern wing in fortress Gothic: thick walls, minimal ornament, defensive towers. His successor Clement VI (politically sophisticated, culturally lavish) built the southern wing in elaborate celebratory Gothic incorporating Italian Renaissance elements — a visible architectural argument about what papal power should project. Together the two wings cover approximately 15,000m²; the Grand Tinel (banqueting hall) alone is 52m long.

The papal apartments were frescoed by the Italian artist Matteo Giovannetti in the 1340s and survive partially intact — some of the most important 14th-century secular painting in France. The palace served as a French army barracks from the Revolution until 1906, during which period some frescoes were damaged and walls were subdivided. Restoration has been ongoing since. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1995 as part of the Historic Centre of Avignon designation.

Logistics
Departs
08:30 · local guide + guide
Route
Palais des Papes interior · Pont Saint-Bénézet · rampart walk
Returns
11:30 · walk back to ship
Aboard by
11:50 — Ship departs 12:00 FIRM
12:00 departure is the hardest deadline of the trip. Guides confirm headcount at 11:30 before beginning the return walk. No exceptions.
AM · Tour 2
Avignon Late Riser — City Overview & Bakery at Les Halles
▾
Why We Go Here
For guests who need a slower morning — or who chose the full walking tour yesterday and want a different pace today. The route still covers Place du Palais and the Pont Saint-Bénézet exterior at a comfortable walking speed, with a proper bakery stop built in. The sacristan pastry is specifically Avignonais — found in this city and almost nowhere else — and Les Halles's living green wall is one of the great urban gestures in modern French architecture. ✦ Magical Moment: the sacristan
The Story
  • The sacristan pastry: a twisted puff pastry stick sprinkled with sugar and sometimes almonds, baked until crisp and golden. Made in Avignon for at least 300 years. The name possibly derives from the sacristain — the church official who managed the building's keys — the twist of the pastry supposedly resembling a bunch of keys. Possibly from convent bakers. No definitive history. Very definitive flavour. ↔ all ages
  • Les Halles d'Avignon: the covered daily market — a genuine local institution, not a tourist market. Fishmongers, cheese vendors, olive oil producers, bakers selling directly to Avignon households and restaurant buyers. The quality standard is maintained by the vendors themselves.
  • Patrick Blanc's living wall: the French botanist who pioneered vertical garden technology designed the exterior of Les Halles. 15,000 plants of 200+ species growing vertically on a hydroponic system with no soil. One of the largest living walls in France. The building is effectively invisible behind it. Blanc's green walls now appear on 300+ buildings worldwide — this one in Avignon was among the early landmark installations.
  • Place du Palais at 09:30: one of the great civic spaces in France, and one of the very few moments in the day when it is quiet. By 11:00 the tour groups arrive and the scale shifts. The early morning square — the Palais turning amber in the first real light, cafés opening, pigeons and silence — is worth going slowly through.
Setting the Stage

The sacristan needs its context before it arrives in hand. One sentence at the bakery counter: "This pastry has been made in this city for at least 300 years. It exists here and almost nowhere else. It is named, possibly, after a man who kept the church keys. Nobody is entirely sure." Then hand it out warm. Specificity is what makes a pastry more than breakfast.

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Living wall species challenge: 200 plant species on the Les Halles exterior wall. How many distinct plants can be identified just by looking? A botanist could name every one. How close can the JAs get?
  • Sacristan shape debate: the pastry is named (possibly) after a bunch of church keys. Hold it up. Does the twisted shape look like keys? What else does it look like? Everyone gets one vote.
  • Place du Palais quiet moment: stand in the centre of the square and face each of the four sides in turn. Name what you see. The Palais, the cathedral, the Petit Palais, the open city. One square, seven centuries of ecclesiastical power.
Rabbit Holes 🐇
🌱 Wikipedia — Patrick Blanc ▾

French botanist born 1953, senior researcher at the CNRS (Centre National de la Recherche Scientifique). Developed the concept of the mur végétal (living wall or vertical garden) in the late 1980s after studying tropical epiphytic plants that grow without soil on vertical surfaces in rainforests — cliff faces, tree trunks, and rock outcrops. His system uses a hydroponic felt layer mounted on a metal frame, through which water and nutrients circulate; plants root into the felt rather than soil.

The Les Halles d'Avignon installation uses approximately 15,000 plants of 200+ species and is one of the largest in France. His earlier landmark installations include the Musée du Quai Branly in Paris (2006) and the Marithé François Girbaud boutique in Paris. His work now appears on buildings in London, Singapore, Madrid, and 300+ other locations. He holds multiple patents on the vertical garden system and continues as head of a research laboratory in Paris.

🏛️ Wikipedia — Avignon ▾

Known in Roman times as Avenio, the city was an important trading post on the Rhône before the Roman conquest. The medieval city walls — 4.3km, 39 towers, 7 gates, largely intact — were built in the 14th century under the Avignon popes to protect the enormous wealth concentrated in the city. They are among the best-preserved medieval urban fortifications in Europe.

The annual Avignon Festival, founded in 1947 by Jean Vilar, is one of the world's largest performing arts festivals — approximately 1,500 shows in July, filling every available outdoor and indoor space in the city including the courtyard of the Palais des Papes. In June, the city has the infrastructure of the festival without the crowds. The Petit Palais (former archbishops' residence on the Place du Palais) houses one of the finest collections of Italian and Avignonese medieval painting in France.

Logistics
Guide ready
09:15 at ship · local guide
Departs
09:30 · max 15 per sub-group
Route
Place du Palais exterior · Pont Saint-Bénézet exterior · Les Halles bakery
Bakery stop
Each guest: one sacristan + drink (coffee/tea/juice/water)
Market free time
15 min to explore Les Halles independently
Aboard by
11:50 — Ship departs 12:00 FIRM
12:00 departure is firm. Bakery stop and free market time must not overrun. Guides manage the clock actively from 11:15 onward.
PM · Tour 1 · from Arles
Arles Introduction Tour — Amphitheatre & Van Gogh Garden
▾
Why We Go Here
Explore a medieval fortress village perched high on a jagged limestone ridge, where ancient stone houses look out over the valley below. Then, transition from the heights into the earth itself by entering the Carrières de Lumière. Inside this colossal, 7,000-square-metre former stone quarry, the massive underground walls, floors, and ceilings are completely transformed by an immersive, projected digital art and music installation.
The Story
  • Arles (Arelate) founded 46 BCE by Julius Caesar as a Roman colony — a reward to the legions who helped defeat Pompey. It grew to 75,000–100,000 people. Constantine the Great used it as a western imperial capital in the early 4th century. The entire historic centre is UNESCO World Heritage. The construction material of the Roman forum, theatre, and cryptoporticus was systematically scavenged for 1,500 years of medieval building — the whole city is a visible archaeological palimpsest.
  • The amphitheatre (70 CE): one of the best-preserved Roman arenas in the world — and one of the very few still used for its original purpose. The Arles Feria (bullfighting festival) fills it to 20,000 twice a year — Easter and September. 2,000 years of public spectacle in the same structure, continuous. ↔ all ages
  • Van Gogh arrived in Arles February 1888 and painted 200+ works in 15 months — one of the most productive creative periods of any artist in history. He was fascinated by the light: "The sun here dyes things with a colour of gold." The ear incident (December 23, 1888) followed an argument with Gauguin.
  • The Espace Van Gogh — the former Hôtel-Dieu hospital — is where Van Gogh was admitted December 24, 1888 and spent weeks recovering. He looked from his room window onto the courtyard garden and painted it. The garden has been restored to match his 1889 canvas exactly: the same flower beds, the same tree, the same arched walkway, the same proportions. Walking into it is walking into a painting that exists in a museum somewhere else.
  • Reading the walls in old Arles: Roman foundations at street level, medieval stonework above, Renaissance and Baroque additions higher still. Every old wall in the city is a vertical cross-section of 2,000 years of habitation. Once you see it, you cannot stop seeing it.
  • LUMA Arles (Frank Gehry, 2021): one of the most significant new cultural institutions in France — a contemporary art centre on a former railway site south of the old town. The stainless steel tower is visible from across the city. It has transformed Arles's cultural profile and drawn a new generation of visitors.
Setting the Stage

The Espace Van Gogh garden is the emotional anchor — and the deliberate setup for Day 7. Standing in it, after establishing the hospital context: "This is the garden Van Gogh looked at from his hospital window in 1888. Tomorrow we drive to where he went next — Saint-Paul de Mausole in Saint-Rémy, where he spent 12 months and made 150 paintings. This garden is where his story in Provence started. Tomorrow we follow where it went." The promise of tomorrow, planted today.

The Garden Moment
A Promise Kept 135 Years Later

The Espace Van Gogh garden was restored to match Van Gogh's painting exactly. Not approximately. Not in the spirit of. Exactly. The same flower beds, the same tree, the same arched walkway. Standing in it while knowing the painting exists somewhere in a museum — and that this garden was brought back from neglect specifically to honour it — is one of those moments where the distance between art and life collapses completely.

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Amphitheatre timeline: this arena was built when Jerusalem's second temple still stood, before London existed, before Christianity was legal in the Roman Empire. Walk in and try to feel how old the stones are. Not think about it — feel it.
  • Van Gogh garden comparison: show a photo of Van Gogh's 1889 painting of the garden. Find the specific tree. Find the specific arch. Find the angle he painted from. Stand in it.
  • Wall reading challenge: in one Arles alley, find where Roman stone ends and medieval masonry begins. How can you tell? (Colour, texture, the way the stones are cut and laid.)
Rabbit Holes 🐇
🎨 Wikipedia — Van Gogh in Arles ▾

Van Gogh arrived in Arles on February 20, 1888, seeking better light, lower costs, and distance from Paris's overstimulating art scene. In 15 months in Arles and Saint-Rémy he produced approximately 900 works. In Arles alone: the Night Café, the Bedroom series, the Sunflowers series, the Café Terrace at Night, the Drawbridge series, and 20 self-portraits. He invited Paul Gauguin to join him in October 1888 for a permanent artists' colony.

Gauguin arrived in late October; the collaboration lasted nine weeks before ending with the incident of December 23, 1888. The most complete historical account of the Arles period is Martin Bailey's Studio of the South (2021), which draws on recently discovered documents to clarify several long-standing misconceptions about the breakdown.

🏟️ Wikipedia — Arles Amphitheatre ▾

Built approximately 70–90 CE during the Flavian dynasty — contemporaneous with the Colosseum in Rome, though smaller (capacity 20,000 vs Rome's 50,000+). Arena floor: 69 × 40 metres. 34 rows of seating in local Fontvieille limestone. During the medieval period the amphitheatre was converted into a fortified village — approximately 200 houses and two churches were built within and upon the structure, with a watchtower added to the exterior wall.

The medieval structures were demolished and the amphitheatre cleared in 1825 to restore its original configuration. It remains in active use for the Arles Feria (bullfighting festival) held at Easter and in September — capacity crowds of 20,000 attend each corrida. It is the best-preserved Roman arena in France in terms of structural continuity and the only one that has been in near-continuous use for its original purpose of public spectacle for 2,000 years.

Logistics
Ship arrives Arles
16:30
Guide ready
17:00 at ship · local guide + guide
Tour
17:30–19:00
Route
Amphitheatre exterior + Espace Van Gogh garden + old town walk
Return walk
19:00–19:30 · back to ship
Guide Destination Brief

Day 7 — Arles

Wednesday 10 June 2026 · Full day ashore · Last full day
📷 #83
Carrières de Lumière — figure silhouetted in projected art, surrounded on all surfaces
Guide Destination Brief

Day 7 — Arles

Wednesday 10 June 2026 · Full day ashore · Last full day
📷 #84
Les Baux-de-Provence on its limestone ridge, morning light, ruined château at far end
Guide Destination Brief

Day 7 — Arles

Wednesday 10 June 2026 · Full day ashore · Last full day
📷 #85
Saint-Paul de Mausole cloister garden — iris beds, stone fountain, cypress above walls
Guide Destination Brief

Day 7 — Arles

Wednesday 10 June 2026 · Full day ashore · Last full day
Day 7 · Arles · Last Full Day
Van Gogh · Les Baux · Carrières · Olive Mill
Day 7 brings us into the stark limestone landscape of the Alpilles, a day defined by sharp contrasts between open-air creativity, vertical medieval history, and subterranean art.

We begin by setting up easels directly within the rugged hillsides that Vincent van Gogh spent a year capturing on canvas, painting the same light and cypress trees. From there, we move to a medieval fortress village perched on a jagged ridge — a place that presents a striking study in scale, maintaining just 22 permanent residents while welcoming 1.5 million visitors a year. Next, we head deep inside the mountain itself, stepping into a massive former rock quarry where the industrial stone walls, floors, and ceilings are completely activated by an immersive, projected digital art installation.

We finish the day grounded in local agriculture, visiting an estate to taste exceptionally fresh olive oil pressed within the last 48 hours right from the surrounding groves.
AM & PM: Van Gogh + PaintingAM & PM: Les Baux + Carrières de LumièreAM & PM: Olive Mill
AM & PM · Tour 1
Van Gogh — Saint-Paul de Mausole & Painting Activity
▾
Why We Go Here
Van Gogh is not a museum subject in the Alpilles. He is a landscape. The countryside around Saint-Rémy is exactly as he painted it — the same cypress trees, the same limestone outcrops, the same silver-green olive groves, the same particular quality of summer light. When guests paint in this landscape using his materials, something happens that no museum visit can replicate. Saint-Paul de Mausole is still a functioning psychiatric hospital. That context is present, and it matters.
The Story
  • Van Gogh arrived in Arles in February 1888 and painted 200+ works in 15 months — one of the most productive creative periods of any artist in recorded history. After the ear incident (December 1888) he voluntarily admitted himself to Saint-Paul de Mausole in Saint-Rémy. He asked for a cell with a window looking onto the garden. He was given it.
  • 12 months at Saint-Paul de Mausole, 150 works: The Starry Night, the cypresses, the olive groves, the wheat fields, the asylum garden — all produced from within these walls, looking at this specific landscape. One painting every 2.4 days on average. Most artists produce 20–30 significant works in a lifetime.
  • He wrote to Théo about the olive trees: "They are very characteristic of this country — silvery, sometimes blue, sometimes green, bronze, whitening on the ground. Very difficult. But oh, so beautiful." He painted 18 olive grove canvases. He never felt he had quite captured the shimmer of the leaves in the Mistral wind. ↔ all ages
  • Saint-Paul de Mausole is a 12th-century Augustinian priory converted to a psychiatric institution in 1807. It still functions as a mental health facility today — patients live and work here. The Romanesque cloister, the current medical mission, and Van Gogh's ghost occupy the same physical space simultaneously.
  • The painting activity: en plein air, in the same landscape Van Gogh worked in, with the same materials — oil or watercolour, canvas or paper, brushes. The point is not to produce a masterpiece. The point is to look at something for long enough to try to describe it. Van Gogh painted the same cypress tree dozens of times. Each time he saw something different.
  • The Espace Van Gogh garden (visited yesterday): the hospital courtyard restored to match his 1889 painting exactly. Saint-Paul de Mausole is the next chapter — the longer stay, the greater productivity, the deeper engagement with this specific Provençal light.
Setting the Stage

Before entering Saint-Paul de Mausole, hold the context. This monastery became a hospital. The hospital treated Van Gogh. Van Gogh made 150 works here in 12 months while in crisis. "We are about to walk into the garden he painted from his window. After that we are going to try to paint what he painted. No cameras until your painting is finished. Look with your eyes first." Then enter. Let the cloister do its work before saying anything else.

The Painting Moment
En Plein Air — Van Gogh's Method, His Landscape, Today

The painting activity is not about technique. It is about looking. Van Gogh painted the same field 15 times. Each time he saw something he'd missed. One hour. One subject. No camera first. Find one thing about this landscape that surprises you and put it on the canvas. The finished work goes home — a painting made in Van Gogh's landscape is a different object from a photograph of it.

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Van Gogh comparison challenge: find the specific cypress, rock formation, or olive tree that appears in one of his paintings. Photograph it from the same angle he painted it from. Compare the two.
  • The shimmer test: Van Gogh wrote that he could never capture the shimmer of olive tree leaves in the Mistral wind. Stand under an olive tree in the breeze. Watch the leaves. In three words — describe what he was trying to paint.
  • No camera until the painting is done. One hour. Look first. Then paint. Then photograph. The sequence matters.
Rabbit Holes 🐇
🎨 Wikipedia — Van Gogh (Saint-Rémy period) ▾

Van Gogh admitted himself to Saint-Paul-de-Mausole on May 8, 1889. He was given a cell and later a second room as a studio, with permission to paint in the grounds and surrounding fields. During 12 months he produced approximately 150 paintings and 100 drawings: The Starry Night (June 1889), the Irises series, 18 olive grove paintings, and extensive copies after Millet and Delacroix. His output averaged one significant work every 2.4 days.

His letters to Théo from this period are among the most detailed primary sources on any artist's working process. He left Saint-Rémy on May 16, 1890 for Auvers-sur-Oise, where he died July 29, 1890. The complete letters are available in English translation (Van Gogh Museum, Amsterdam, 2009) and freely searchable at vangoghletters.org.

🏥 Wikipedia — Saint-Paul de Mausole ▾

A former Augustinian priory founded in the 12th century; the Romanesque cloister and chapel date from the same period. Converted to a private psychiatric institution in 1807. It has operated continuously as a mental health facility since — one of the longest-running psychiatric institutions in France. Today it treats approximately 50 patients in a residential setting and employs art therapy as part of its therapeutic programme.

The Romanesque cloister and chapel are open to the public; patient residential areas are private. The garden visible from the public areas has been partially restored to match Van Gogh's paintings of the asylum grounds. The Espace Van Gogh in Arles (Day 6) is the prior chapter — the hospital where he was treated after the ear incident before transferring to Saint-Rémy.
