AM & PM · Tour 2
Les Baux-de-Provence & Carrières de Lumière
▾
Why We Go Here
Les Baux-de-Provence is one of the most extraordinary medieval sites in France — a village built entirely on and from a white limestone ridge of the Alpilles, with the ruined castle of the Lords of Baux at its far end. Below it, in the Val d'Enfer, the Carrières de Lumière: a former limestone quarry converted into the most dramatic immersive art installation in Europe. The quarry below and the village above are made of the same stone — the village was literally quarried from what is now the exhibition space.
The Story
  • The Lords of Baux — the dynasty from Suze la Rousse on Day 4 — built this village and controlled it from the 10th century. At their peak they held 79 towns across Provence and the Drôme and claimed descent from Balthazar, one of the Three Magi. Louis XIII destroyed the castle with cannon fire in 1632 because they had become too powerful to leave standing. The 16-pointed star carved into stonework at Suze la Rousse — look for it here too.
  • The village today: one of the Plus Beaux Villages de France, approximately 22 full-time residents but 1.5 million visitors per year. Entirely built from and on the local limestone — the buildings are the colour of the rock they sit on. The medieval upper quarter is car-free. ↔ all ages
  • The Carrières de Lumière: former limestone quarry — the stone that built the village above was quarried here. Jean Cocteau used it as a film location in 1960 for Le Testament d'Orphée (cast including Picasso, Jean Marais, and Yul Brynner). Culturespaces transformed it into a digital art installation space in 2012.
  • The technology: approximately 40 high-powered laser projectors, 7,000m² of projection surface covering floor, walls, and ceiling simultaneously, 13 channels of spatial audio. The exhibition runs as a continuous 40-minute loop. Guests walk freely through the projected images — no fixed route, no barriers.
  • The exhibition changes annually. Confirm the June 2026 show before departure — past exhibitions include Klimt, Cézanne, Gaudí, Van Gogh/Vermeer, Miró. Production of each show takes 18 months.
  • 750,000 visitors per year make the Carrières one of the most visited cultural sites in Provence. It opened in 2012 and immediately became the region's defining new attraction.
  • Temperature inside: ~16–18°C year-round — noticeably cooler than June outside, comfortable but worth noting. A light layer is welcome after time in the Alpilles heat.
Setting the Stage

Two threads to bring in before Les Baux: first, the Lords of Baux connection to Day 4's Suze la Rousse. "The castle we visited on Day 4 was their northern stronghold. This village was their home. Louis XIII destroyed the main fortress with cannon fire in 1632 because they'd become too powerful to leave intact. Look at the ruins at the end of the village — that's what too much power looks like." Then for the Carrières: "The stone that makes this village was quarried from the chambers below it. The quarry that carved the village became the space we're about to enter."

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Shadow play in the Carrières: walk in front of the projections — your shadow cuts through the artwork and becomes part of it. Move slowly. Watch what the shadow does to the image.
  • Floor discovery: lie on the floor of the Carrières and look straight up at the ceiling projection. Almost no adults do this. The ceiling view is the best view in the building.
  • Find the 16-pointed star in Les Baux: the Lords of Baux carved their heraldic symbol — the Star of Bethlehem — into stonework across everything they built. The same star appears at Suze la Rousse. Find it here.
Rabbit Holes 🐇
🎬 Wikipedia — Le Testament d'Orphée ▾

Jean Cocteau's final film (1960), written, directed by, and starring Cocteau himself as "the poet." Filmed primarily at the Carrières de Lumières (then called the Cathédrale d'Images) and at Cocteau's home and several Provençal locations. The cast included Pablo Picasso, Jean Marais, Yul Brynner, Charles Aznavour, and Brigitte Bardot in cameo appearances. Cocteau was 70 at the time of filming and in declining health; he died October 11, 1963.

The film is a meditation on the nature of art, time, and the artist's mortality — described by Cocteau as his "last will and testament." The limestone quarry appears as the underworld through which the poet travels, its cavernous geometry and acoustic properties making it, as Cocteau described it, "the most naturally dreamlike place I have ever filmed." It remains one of the last significant works of French cinematic surrealism.

🏰 Wikipedia — Les Baux-de-Provence ▾

The village occupies a white limestone spur of the Alpilles at 200m altitude — built on and from the same rock formation it stands on, making the buildings visually indistinguishable from the terrain at certain angles. Population: approximately 22 permanent residents, 1.5 million annual visitors — one of the highest tourist-to-resident ratios of any village in France. Classified as a Plus Beau Village de France.

The mineral bauxite was named after Les Baux when it was discovered in the area in 1821 by geologist Pierre Berthier — the first aluminium ore identified, from which all subsequent aluminium production derives. The Lords of Baux castle was demolished by cannon in 1632 on the orders of Louis XIII; the ruins cover the far end of the spur. The chapel of the White Penitents (17th century) in the lower village contains decorations by painter Yves Brayer. The Carrières de Lumières is located in the Val d'Enfer (Valley of Hell) directly below the village.

AM & PM · Tour 3
Moulin du Calanquet — Alpilles Olive Mill & Tasting
▾
Why We Go Here
Travel into the heart of the Valley of Les Baux to discover the realities of traditional olive farming. You'll walk through rows of century-old groves, see the mechanics of the modern cold-press mill, and participate in a guided tasting of the latest harvest. You'll learn to identify the natural traits of premium oil, including the signature peppery finish that indicates absolute freshness and purity.
The Story
  • Greek colonists introduced the olive to Provence around 600 BCE — the same civilisation that founded Marseille. Roman expansion dramatically increased production. Provence was Rome's most productive olive oil region outside Italy. The olive trees in this valley today are the cultural descendants of trees planted by the same civilisation that built the Pont du Gard.
  • AOP Vallée des Baux de Provence: one of France's most restrictive agricultural appellations — only 4 communes in the Alpilles qualify. Must use specific local varieties, cold-pressed within 48 hours of harvest, tested for acidity and purity. The Alpilles microclimate — dry summers, limestone soil, intense sun, Mistral wind — stresses the trees into producing oils of extraordinary concentration. ↔ all ages
  • The four Alpilles varieties: Salonenque (mild, buttery, gentle fruit); Aglandau (more intense, green olive notes, slight pepper, good structure); Grossane (round, mild, primarily table olives); Béruguette (aromatic, complex, slight almond notes). Most mills blend at least 2 — the assemblage is the mill's signature.
  • Professional olive oil tasting: appearance (colour from pale gold to deep green); nose (fresh grass, green tomato, artichoke, almond, pepper); palate (sweetness at the front, bitterness in the middle, pungency at the back of the throat). The peppery cough is called the oleocanthal effect — a marker of high polyphenol content and genuine quality oil. A cough is a compliment.
  • Van Gogh painted at least 18 olive grove canvases from Saint-Paul de Mausole, 4km from here. He wrote to Théo: "The olive trees are very characteristic of this country — silvery, sometimes blue, sometimes green, bronze... the shimmer... very difficult. Oh, so beautiful." The trees in the Calanquet grove are the same species, the same landscape.
  • Some Alpilles olive trees are believed to be 700–1,000 years old — planted in the medieval period and still bearing fruit. The oldest known living olive tree on earth (in Crete) is approximately 3,000 years old and still produces olives.
Setting the Stage

Stop at the oldest trees before the tasting. Let everyone look at them — gnarled, low, seemingly modest. Then give them the age: planted before World War II. Survived everything since. Still producing. The oil in the tasting room was pressed 48 hours ago from fruit grown on a tree that was already old when your grandparents were born. That scale lands physically when you're standing in front of the tree, not intellectually when you're reading about it.

The Oleocanthal Moment
The Cough Is a Compliment

During the tasting, if guests feel a peppery, slightly burning sensation at the back of their throat — address it immediately and directly: "That sensation is the oleocanthal effect. It means the oil is fresh, high in polyphenols, and nutritionally exceptional. Industrial olive oils don't produce this — they're processed in ways that destroy those compounds. A cough is applause. The better the oil, the stronger the cough."

JA Fun Bits
JA Fun Bits
  • Olive maths: 5–6 kg of olives to make 1 litre of oil. A mature tree produces 20–40 kg per year. How many trees does the mill need to produce enough oil for one day of production?
  • Scratch-and-sniff: pick an olive leaf, scratch the surface with a fingernail, smell. That green, slightly bitter, herby scent — this is why the entire region south of here smells the way it does.
  • Van Gogh shimmer: look up at olive tree leaves in the Mistral wind. He painted this 18 times and still felt he'd never captured it. Three words: what does it actually look like to you?
Rabbit Holes 🐇
🫒 Wikipedia — Olive oil ▾

Production of olive oil in the Mediterranean basin dates to approximately 6000 BCE in the Levant. The technology reached the Greek colonies of southern France (including Massalia/Marseille) around 600 BCE and was dramatically expanded under Roman occupation — Pliny the Elder described Provençal oil as among the finest in the empire. The cold-pressing method (pressing olive paste at temperatures below 27°C to preserve aromatic compounds) has been the quality standard since antiquity; the basic physics have not changed in 2,500 years.

The International Olive Council classifies olive oil by free acidity and sensory assessment; extra virgin (the highest grade) must have free acidity below 0.8% and no sensory defects. The polyphenol oleocanthal — responsible for the peppery throat sensation in fresh high-quality oil — has been shown in multiple studies to have anti-inflammatory properties analogous to ibuprofen. High-polyphenol oils from early-harvest, cold-pressed production have the strongest oleocanthal effect; the compound degrades with time and processing.

💊 Wikipedia — Oleocanthal ▾

A phenolic compound (deacetoxy-ligstroside aglycon) naturally occurring in fresh extra-virgin olive oil. First isolated and identified in 2005 by Gary Beauchamp and colleagues at the Monell Chemical Senses Center in Philadelphia, who noticed that freshly pressed olive oil produced the same throat irritation sensation as liquid ibuprofen. Subsequent analysis confirmed structural similarity and equivalent inhibition of COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes — the mechanism of action of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs.

Oleocanthal is produced by the olive during ripening and is highly sensitive to processing: high-temperature extraction, filtering, and ageing all significantly degrade concentration. High-polyphenol oils from early-harvest, cold-pressed, unfiltered production — such as those from the AOP Vallée des Baux — typically have the strongest oleocanthal effect. The peppery cough sensation correlates directly with polyphenol concentration and is used by professional tasters as a quality indicator. Oil should ideally be used within 12–18 months of pressing for maximum polyphenol content.

Guide Destination Brief

Day 8 — Arles

Thursday 11 June 2026 · Departure Day · Au Revoir
Day 8 · Au Revoir
⚑ Luggage out 07:30 · Coaches from 09:00
Every great adventure earns its ending.

This morning, as we head south, the Rhône spreads out into the vast open wetlands of the Camargue. The stone walls and steep valleys of the northern river fade away, replaced by flat expanses, white horses, wild flamingos, and the river quietly becoming the Mediterranean Sea.

Eight days. 300 kilometres. From Rome's ancient capital of Gaul to the brilliant light of Van Gogh's Provence.
Departure Day
Last Morning & Coach Talking Points
▾
Talking Points — Morning & Coach
  • The numbers, end to end: 8 days · 300km · 2 rivers (Saône to Rhône) · 7 nights on the AmaKristina · Lyon to Arles. The trip is geographically coherent — you really did travel from one end of a region to the other.
  • What changed between Day 1 and Day 8: Lyon was granite hills and traboules; Arles is limestone and open light. The further south you go, the flatter and wider the land becomes — the Rhône spreading into the delta. The shift from enclosed, wooded gorges to open sky is the landscape story of the week. ↔ all ages
  • The thread they may not have noticed: iron. The same mineral that makes the ochre at Roussillon orange is what makes the Aven d'Orgnac Red Chamber red, gives the Hermitage granite its character, and tints the soil in the Camargue. The whole route sits on different expressions of the same geology.
  • What the Rhône actually is: one of Europe's last partially wild rivers — still floods, still silts, still shifts its banks. The engineers who built the Pont du Gard were taming a tributary of this same river 2,000 years ago. It hasn't been fully tamed since. ↔ all ages
  • Lyon → Arles in one sentence: from the city that fed France to the city that lit up Van Gogh. Everything in between was the story of how the south of France became the south of France.
  • For families with kids: ask them what they'd put in a time capsule from the trip. Not the best photo — the one object, smell, sound, or flavour they'd want to remember in 20 years. The answers are always better than anyone expects.
  • The Camargue, visible from the coach heading south: the Rhône splits into the Grand Rhône and Petit Rhône just south of Arles. Everything between those two branches is the Camargue delta — 930km² of protected wetland. White horses, black bulls, pink flamingos, 400 bird species. The river doesn't end at Arles. It opens. ↔ all ages
✕ ‹ ›
